Isaac and Rebecca travel to Gerar, where Isaac makes a peace treaty with King Abimelech. Isaac gives Jacob the blessing meant for Esau, and Jacob runs away to his uncle Laban.

aliases: The Counting of the Omer Barley Offering
sefirat-haomer.bio: Sefirat HaOmer (literally, "the counting of the Omer") is the period of 49 days between Passover and Shavuot. Each day is verbally counted off. Traditionally, this period of time is a solemn one, and many avoid celebrations during this calendar period.

elul.bio: Elul is the 12th month of the Hebrew calendar and is a time during which the liturgy focuses on preparation to stand before God in judgement on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.

gehinnom.bio: Gehenna, or *Gehinnom* in Hebrew, is often thought of as the Jewish hell and is the term used in rabbinic literature to refer to the place where the wicked go after death. The name likely originates from a valley south of Jerusalem called Gehinnom or Gey Ben Hinnom, which the prophet Jeremiah [cursed](https://www.sefaria.org/Jeremiah.19.2?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) for being a site of idolatry and child sacrifice.

parashat-tetzaveh.bio: Tetzaveh (“You Shall Command”) opens as God instructs Moses to appoint Aaron and his sons as priests. God details how to make the priestly clothing, how to sanctify the priests and offer sacrifices during the seven days of inauguration in the Mishkan (Tabernacle), and how to build the golden altar.

torah-scrolls.bio: A Torah scroll (*sefer Torah* in Hebrew) is a copy of the Torah written by hand on parchment according to various rules to ensure its accuracy, readability, and holiness. It is primarily used in synagogues during Jewish prayer services.

parashat-kedoshim.bio: Kedoshim (“Holy”) opens by instructing the Israelites to be holy. It details dozens of laws regulating all aspects of life, including observing Shabbat, loving one’s neighbor, and leaving portions of a field for the poor. It ends by detailing punishments for certain types of idolatry and sexual misconduct.

synagogues.bio: The synagogue (beit knesset, meaning “house of assembly”) serves as a spiritual center for Jewish communities around the world. Because of their role as houses of prayer, there are many laws about how synagogues should be built, as well as what is allowed in and around them.

giving.bio: Jewish textual tradition values giving and [generosity](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/generosity?tab=admin). The Torah instructs providing for those in need and requires the distribution of agricultural gifts to the poor, the priests, and the Levites. Later Jewish texts elaborate on these commandments and on the importance of supporting communal institutions.

covenants.bio: Covenants are binding commitments made between two parties. The most famous covenants in Tanakh are those that God made with Abraham, these include the ["covenant between the parts"](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.15.2?lang=bi&aliyot=0)  and that of [circumcision](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/circumcision?tab=sources). People too can make covenants, as was the case when Abraham made [one with Abimelekh](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.21.23?lang=bi&aliyot=0).

rabbi-yitzhak-b-nahman.bio: R. Yitzchak b. Nachman was a student of Shmuel in Nehardea, and then moved to Israel where he studied under R. Yehoshua B. Levi.

trust.bio: "[He put his trust in God](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.15.6?lang=bi&vside=THE_JPS_TANAKH:_Gender-Sensitive_Edition|en&with=Translation%20Open&lang2=en)," the Torah states of Abram (later to be called Abraham), as God shows him the stars and promises that one day his descendants will be so numerous that they'll be just as uncountable. This is perhaps the first moment of explicit trust in the Torah, and Abraham is often discussed as a paragon of trust in God in later Jewish texts. Many other biblical texts speak of trust in moments when help didn't feel immediately tangible, and works of Jewish thought and Musar discuss trust extensively, including its connection to knowledge of God, the balance between trust and action, and how to practically develop and grow one's trust.

parashat-tzav.bio: In Tzav (“Command”), God tells Moses about the sacrifices offered in the Tabernacle (portable sanctuary), including a meal offering brought by the high priest, guilt offerings, and offerings of thanks. Moses initiates Aaron and Aaron’s sons for priestly service in the Tabernacle.

parashat-chukat.bio: Chukat (“Law Of”) opens by describing the process of burning the red heifer and using its ashes for purification. It also tells the stories of the deaths of Miriam and Aaron, Moses’ striking of a rock to bring forth water, a plague of venomous snakes, and battles against the Emorite kings Sihon and Og.

snakes1.bio: The snake appears from the opening narratives of the Torah, as a snake [entices](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.3.1?lang=bi&aliyot=0) Eve to eat from the tree of knowledge. Various other biblical verses mention snakes as part of stories, prophecies, and metaphors. Rabbinic texts discuss the dangerous nature of the snake and different laws surrounding snakes, like the [permissibility](https://www.sefaria.org/Shabbat.121b.4?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) of killing them on Shabbat.

rabbi-berekhyah.bio: Rabbi Berekhiyah was a student of Rav Helbo, and he brings down many of his teachings. Most of this teachings are homiletical.

kelippot1.bio: See section 83, where it is explained that spiritual beings are separated and divided from one another by their difference of form. Know that divergent forms, from one extreme to the other, separates these beings and distances them from one extreme to its opposite, until there is no suckling between them at all. As you already know, the supernal light contains only a will to give, not to receive anything, whereas the kelippot have the exact opposite forms: Only a will to receive and to swallow everything; they have no will to give whatsoever. Therefore, they are separated from the Source of Life and are called dead, they and all that cleave to them. This must be understood (Talmud Eser Sefirot, v. 3, Q & A 88).

parashat-vaera.bio: Vaera (“I Appeared”) opens as God promises to redeem the enslaved Israelites and bring them to the Promised Land. When Pharaoh repeatedly refuses to let the Israelites go, God sends a series of plagues: water turning to blood, frogs, lice, wild animals, death of livestock, boils, and hail.

books.bio: The Jewish people are often called "the people of the Book." While "the Book" in the phrase at hand refers to the Tanakh, the phrase also reflects the centrality of books and the [study of Jewish texts](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/torah-study?tab=admin) in Judaism. Within traditional Jewish literature, much is written on the value and nature of books: Halakhic texts discuss how to treat sacred books, rabbinic texts explore the authorship of different biblical books, and works of Jewish thought and Kabbalah consider the impact of text study on the mind and soul.

aliases: Yehoyakim b. Josia the King of Judah
Jehoiakim b. Josiah, king of Judah
jehoiakim.bio: Jehoiakim was the 18th king of Judah. Originally an Egyptian vassal, he switched allegiance to Babylonia in the hopes of preventing Babylon from destroying Jerusalem. In rabbinic literature, he is portrayed as a very wicked king.

shimon-hatzaddik.bio: Shimon the Righteous was a high priest and teacher at a time of political flux in the region. He is credited with meeting Alexander the Great and convincing him not to attack Jerusalem. He was the first teacher mentioned in Pirkei Avot and among the last surviving members of the Great Assembly, a Rabbinic legislative body that existed in the Second Temple period .

adams-sin-and-his-punishment.canonical: Eating From the Tree of Knowledge
aliases: Eating From the Tree of Knowledge
adams-sin-and-his-punishment.bio: Upon placing the first human in the [garden of Eden](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/garden-of-eden?tab=sources), God immediately commands him not to eat from the [tree of knowledge of good and evil](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-tree-of-knowledge?tab=sources), lest he die. Several verses later, in [Genesis 3](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.3.1?lang=bi&with=Rashi&lang2=en), after God has created [Adam's](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/adam?tab=sources) counterpart, [Eve](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/eve?tab=sources), a [snake](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/snakes1?tab=sources) convinces her to partake of the forbidden fruit, which she in turn shares with Adam. And while Adam, Eve, and the snake are not punished with death, they are punished.

sleep.bio: Sleep features from the outset of the Bible, as God [casts](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.2.21?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) a deep sleep on Adam when God creates Eve. Other biblical texts incorporate sleep as well – as a [step](https://www.sefaria.org/Judges.4.21?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) in a plot, as a metaphor for laziness or spiritual disengagement, or as a [precursor](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.28.12?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) to divinely-inspired dreams. Rabbinic and kabbalistic texts later discuss the spiritual significance of sleep and dreams, as well as halakhic questions related to sleep, such as the parameters for the obligation to sleep in the sukkah. Jewish liturgy incorporates prayers to be recited upon going to sleep and upon waking up in the mo

rav-ketina.bio: Rav Ketina was a highly respected teacher in Babylonia and his teachings are brought down by several rabbis such as Rav Chisda.

sotah.bio: Sotah is a halakhic term for a woman suspected of adultery.

incense.bio: Incense (Ketoret) was offered daily on the Golden Altar in the Mishkan and Temple, and its ingredients are dictated in the Torah. There are also mystical properties associated with it, as seen from its use in protecting Jews from God's wrath in the desert (Num. 17).

bread.bio: Bread is considered a staple of life in the Jewish textual tradition. Biblical figures offer bread to their guests as a marker of hospitality, and the sacred showbread plays a role in the rituals of the Tabernacle and Temple. [Many laws](https://www.sefaria.org/Jerusalem_Talmud_Challah.1.6.3?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) govern the preparation, baking, and consumption of bread. Bread is unique in that its consumption necessitates reciting the full [Birkat HaMazon](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/birkat-hamazon?tab=admin) (Grace After Meals).

parashat-ki-tavo.bio: Ki Tavo (“When You Come”) opens by describing the ceremony of the first fruit offering (bikkurim) and the declaration made upon the completion of tithing. It concludes with a detailed description of blessings that follow obedience to God's laws and curses that come with their desecration.

birkat-hamazon.bio: [Birkat HaMazon](https://www.sefaria.org/Siddur_Ashkenaz%2C_Berachot%2C_Birkat_HaMazon?lang=bi), known in English as "Grace After Meals" is the series of four blessings recited after a meal in which has eaten bread. While all other blessings are commanded by the rabbis, proclaiming thanks to God after a meal is a commandment from the Torah itself, in [Deuteronomy 8:10](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.8.10?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en).

hallel.bio: [Hallel](https://www.sefaria.org/Siddur_Ashkenaz%2C_Festivals%2C_Rosh_Chodesh%2C_Hallel%2C_Berakhah_before_the_Hallel?lang=en) consists of Psalms 113–118 and is recited on most holidays. There are, however, some holidays on which only part or none of it is recited. These chapters are psalms of thanksgiving and focus on God's communal and personal salvation for those who put their trust in God.

priestly-blessing.bio: The [priestly blessing](https://www.sefaria.org/Siddur_Ashkenaz%2C_Weekday%2C_Shacharit%2C_Amidah%2C_Birkat_Kohanim.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) — birkat kohanim in Hebrew —  is a blessing recited by kohanim (those of priestly descent) unto the congregation. Its origins are in [Numbers 6:24–27](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.6.24?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en), and these verses are the blessing itself. In Israel, the blessing is recited as part of daily service. Outside of Israel, customs vary as to when it is recited.

rabbi-nehunya-b-hakanah.bio: R. Nechunya b. haKanah also lived during the time of R. Yochanan ben Zakkai. He directed his own academy and  authored several prayers and laws. Highly respected by his peers, he attributed his unusually advanced age to his harmonious relations with others.

og.bio: Og was the Amorite king of Bashan who was defeated by Moses and the Israelites in the Book of Numbers. In Jewish tradition he is understood to have been a giant.

partzuf1.bio: The ten [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) that are one below the other, which result from the ascent of the [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) to the Creator, are called [*partzufim*](/topics/partzuf) (as stated in *Histaklut Penimit* 8:13, section 1) (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 3, Q & A 83).

sanhedrin.bio: The *sanhedrin* ("assembly") was a legislative and judicial assembly of either 23 or 70 elders, existing at both a local and central level in the ancient Land of Israel.

rabbi-elazar-b-yose.bio: R. Elazar b. Yosi is from the later teachers in the Land of Israel. He was a colleague of R. Yose b. Abun.

parashat-vayikra.bio: In Vayikra (“He Called”), the first Torah portion in the book of Leviticus, God tells Moses about the sacrifices offered in the Mishkan (Tabernacle). Among these are sacrifices entirely burnt on the altar, meal offerings made of flour and oil, peace offerings, and sacrifices brought for sinning inadvertently.

parashat-behar.bio: Behar (“On The Mountain”) details the laws of the sabbatical year (*shemittah*), when working the land is prohibited and debts are forgiven. It also sets out laws of indentured servitude and of the jubilee year (*yovel*), when property reverts to its original ownership.

ethics.bio: Cases and discussion of ethics abound throughout the Jewish textual tradition, as texts aim to instruct and analyze how to live a life of moral principles.

conversion.bio: Conversion refers to the process through which non-Jews may become Jewish. The concept is often associated with the biblical character [Ruth](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/ruth), a woman of Moabite origin who is described as "[taking refuge](https://www.sefaria.org/Ruth.2.12?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi) under the wings of God's presence." Rabbinic sources see this as an indicator of Ruth's conversion and interpret details about the laws of conversion from Ruth's story.

parashat-bechukotai.bio: Bechukotai (“In My Laws”), the final Torah portion in the book of Leviticus, describes blessings that follow obedience to God's laws and curses that come with desecration of them. It ends with the laws of vows and consecration of people and property.

second-temple.bio: After the First Temple (Beit Hamikdash) was destroyed by the Babylonians and the Jews went into exile, the Second Temple (Bayit Sheni) was built by returning exiles with the permission of the new Persian rulers. It was not nearly as elaborate as the First Temple.

mikhal.bio: Mikhal was a daughter of King Saul who also becomes the wife of King David. When her father seeks to kill her husband, she helps David escape. According to rabbinic midrash, she only gave birth to a child on the day of her death, suggesting she died in childbirth.

mikveh.bio: The mikveh is a pool (literally, a “gathering”) of water from a natural source. While the Temples stood, to gain entry into the Temple, to partake of certain sacrifices, or for a couple to be intimate — among other reasons — Jews tried to maintain a status of ritual purity. While there are different kinds of ritual impurity and as many processes to regain a status of ritual purity, immersion in a mikveh, a ritual bath, is an important element in the purification process. Today, the practice of immersion in a mikveh is used to mark life transitions, conversion to Judaism, to ready oneself for Yom Kippur, before birth, and as a central element in the observance of the laws of niddah (the practices surrounding menstruation, intimacy, and ritual purity).

parashat-miketz.bio: Mikketz (“After”) follows Joseph as he interprets Pharaoh's dreams and rises to become second-in-command to Pharaoh. When Joseph’s brothers come to Egypt seeking food during a famine, Joseph accuses them of spying. He insists that they return with their youngest brother, Benjamin, and later plants a goblet in Benjamin’s bag.

batsheva3.bio: Batsheva was one of King David's wives. He saw her bathing on a rooftop and arranged to have her husband sent to the front and killed so he could marry her. Their son Solomon succeeded King David, though he had many older brothers.

ramban.bio: Moshe ben Nachman, also known as Ramban, was a leading Torah scholar of the middle ages who authored commentaries on Torah and the Talmud. He was a posek who wrote responsa and stand-alone works on Halachic topics, as well as works on mysticism, science and philosophy. Ramban's commentary on the Torah often critiques earlier commentaries and incorporates kabbalistic teachings. He was born in Gerona, Spain, where he established a large yeshiva which produced hundreds of disciples who became leaders of Spanish Jewry. In 1263 he took part in a debate in Barcelona with an apostate Jew named Pablo Christiani, at the behest of the Church. In 1267, at the age of 72, he immigrated to the Holy Land, where he settled in Akko (Acre). He died there at age 76.

partition2.bio: The power of [constriction](/topics/constriction) ([*tzimtzum*](/topics/tzimtzum1)) and judgment instituted in [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), which rebuffs the supernal light from [enclothing](/topics/enclothe) inside it (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 3, Q & A 63).

samuel-(prophet).bio: The prophet Samuel (Shmuel in Hebrew) led the Israelites before the King Saul, the first king, was anointed. Under his leadership, Israel rose up against the Phillistines. He anointed Saul as king and advised him before secretly anointing David when God decided Saul was no longer fit to be king.

eve.bio: Eve was the first woman, the "mother of all," created by being separated from [Adam's](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/adam?tab=sources) body. In the [garden of Eden](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/garden-of-eden?tab=sources), the snake convinced Eve to try the forbidden fruit of the [tree of knowledge](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-tree-of-knowledge?tab=sources), from which she also gave to Adam. The result was that they were both expelled from the garden and that she and all of womankind would be cursed to endure difficult [childbirths](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/birth?tab=sources) even as they desire their husbands, to whom they are subject.

aliases: Rabbi Hanina the High Priest's deputy
Rabbi Hanina the deputy High Priest
Rabbi Hanina the vice-chief of the priests
Rabbi Hanina, the vice-chief of priests
Rabbi Hanina, chief of the priests
Rabbi Hanina the vice-chief of priests
Rabbi Hananiah the chief of the priests
Rabbi Hananya, chief of the priests
Rabbi Hanina, the deputy High Priest
Rabbi Hanina vice-chief of the priests
Rebbi Hanania the executive officer of the Cohanim
Rebbi Hananiah, the executive officer of the Cohanim
Rebbi Hanina the executive officer of the Cohanim
Rebbi Hananiah the executive officer of the Cohanim
rabbi-hanina-segan-hakohanim.bio: R. Chanina Deputy of the Priests was an influential scholar at the time of Rabban Shimon b. Gamliel. As a priest, he was very involved in determining and preserving the laws of the priestly Temple service. He is reputed to have been martyred by the Romans.

dogs.bio: Dogs first feature in the Torah in the book of Exodus, when, during the final plague in Egypt, [God ensures](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.11.7?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) that not even a dog would snarl at the people of Israel. Later in the same book, the Torah instructs that certain meat that is forbidden for human consumption be [given to dogs](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.22.30?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Rabbinic sources explore the connection between these two references to canines. In other biblical, rabbinic, halakhic, and mystical texts, dogs feature in many negative contexts — for example, they are often depicted as feeding on corpses and as dangerous to humans. Still, other sources highlight positive qualities of dogs, such as their ability to sense the unseen

naomi.bio: Naomi is one of the chief figures of the Book of Ruth. She was Ruth's mother-in-law who moved to Moab with her family during a famine, only to return to her home in Bethlehem with Ruth after her husband and sons died. Despondent and poor, she found hope again when Ruth married Boaz and bore a son.

parashat-pekudei.bio: Pekudei (“Accountings Of”) is the final Torah reading in the book of Exodus. It describes the making of priestly garments worn in the Mishkan (Tabernacle) and the completion of its construction. At God’s command, Moses erects the Mishkan and puts its vessels in place, and God's presence fills the Mishkan.

rosh-chodesh.bio: Rosh Chodesh (the new month)  is celebrated for either one or two days. Before the calendar was set, this was determined by whether the new moon was seen on the 30th of the month. The Torah instituted that each new month be commemorated with a sacrifice. While no animals are currently sacrificed, many mark Rosh Chodesh with extra singing and prayers, including [Hallel](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/hallel?tab=sources), [Torah reading](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.28?lang=bi&aliyot=0), and a [haftarah](https://www.sefaria.org/Isaiah.66?lang=bi). In some communities, women gather together and refrain from work in honor of the womens' refusal to contribute to the making of the golden calf.

babel.bio: Located in ancient Mesopotamia, Babel was the capital city of the Babylonian empire and is notable as the location of the Tower of Babel. Although the Babylonian empire destroyed the Temple in Jerusalem, the subsequent exile ultimately resulted in the creation of the Babylonian Talmud.

rahab.bio: Rahab was a Canaanite woman, described in the biblical text as a prostitute, who lived in the walls of Jericho. She hid two Israelite spies in her home, and in return, the Israelites spared her and her family when they conquered Jericho. Rabbinic tradition teaches that she converted, and she and [Joshua](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/joshua?tab=admin) married and had a family with prominent offspring. Mystical and chasidic interpretations discuss the power of her repentance and the symbolism of her crimson cord.

parashat-achrei-mot.bio: Acharei Mot (“After The Death”) opens by describing the ritual service of Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement. It then details the prohibitions of offering sacrifices outside of the Tabernacle and of eating animal blood and ends with a list of forbidden sexual relations.

parashat-metzora.bio: Metzora opens by describing the purification process and accompanying sacrifices for one infected with *tzara'at*, a discoloration condition on the skin, clothing, or house. It then describes the process of treating a house infected with *tzara'at* and the ritual impurity generated by certain bodily discharges.

safek.bio: "Safek" is a term originating from Rabbinic literature and is not found in the Bible itself. In legal contexts, when uncertainty arises, approaches vary depending on whether the commandment in question originates from the Torah or from Rabbinic sources.

beria.bio: The world of *Beria* is the second of the four worlds (after the supernal world of *Adam Kadmon*) that emanate and descend from another in the process of creation. According to one explanation, its name is derived from the Hebrew word *bar*, a term that connotes that which is external, implying that the world of *Beria* is a garment for the world of [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut). The world of *Beria* parallels the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), or the second [level](/topics/level) of creation, and it is the beginning point of the [enclothing](/topics/enclothe) of [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut) into a reality that created beings can receive. The world of *Beria* is also called the world of the throne, signifying the Divine lowering itself onto a throne, so to speak, to

kiddush-hashem.bio: Usually a reference to religious martyrdom.

priestly-garments.bio: The priestly garments (in Hebrew, bigdei kehunah) were special clothing, primarily made of linen, that were worn by the priests (kohanim) during their service in the [mishkan](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=sources) and later in the Temple. In the book of Exodus, the artisans [Betzalel](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/betzalel?tab=sources) and Oholiav are instructed to make the special garments, which are meant to serve as symbols of "[honor and glory](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.28.2?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en)."

akavyah-b-mahalalel.bio: Akavia was an independent and highly principled scholar who was offered the position of head of court on condition that he would rescind his rulings. He refused and it appears that he was excommunicated as a result.

power.bio: Power is often understood as a demonstrable, physical strength. However, an inner power of restraint is also seen as a sign of strength of character. The Jewish textual tradition frequently regards God as all-powerful, and this divine power is contrasted with the lesser power of mere mortals. The power expressed through ongoing creation, nature, and the world is presented in various genres as an emanation of God’s power.

dinah.bio: Dinah was the daughter of the patriarch [Jacob](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/jacob?tab=sources). When she goes out to "visit the daughters of the land," she encounters the prince of Shechem, named Shechem, who takes her by force, rapes her, falls in love with her, and negotiates with her family to marry her. Dismayed upon hearing what has happened and undermining their father's negotiations, Dinah's brothers Simeon and Levi take matters into their own hands and slaughter Shechem and all the men of the city and return their sister to her family.

rabbi-nehorai.bio: While R. Nehorai may be a second name for one of the other scholars, many citations use this name. Most of these citations are homiletic and some of them come in the context of his discussions with the prophet, Eliyahu.

yerovam-(son-of-nebat).bio: Yerovam was the first king of the northern Kingdom of Israel after revolting against the rule of the Davidic dynasty. In Israel they set up their own priesthood and temple in opposition to the Judean priesthood and temple.

choni-hameagel1.bio: Choni lived in the time of Shimon b. Shetach and was known for his great piety and scholarship. Several spiritual feats are associated with him, such as drawing a circle in the sand and refusing to move until God produced proper rain. Another story recounts his going to sleep for 70 years.

sherets.bio: *Sheratzim* are small creatures that creep or crawl, described and listed in Leviticus [11:29-30](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.11.31?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Their corpses convey impurity to people and vessels when encountered in specific ways.

rabbi-pinchas-b-yair.bio: R. Pinchas was the son-in-law of R. Shimon bar Yochai and lived during the time of R. Yehudah haNasi. He was most famous for his outstanding piety, especially in the area of tithes, such that even his donkey was stringent about them. He is also well known for his ladder of virtues, later immortalized by R. Moshe Hayyim Luzzatto.

noah-and-the-ark.bio: Noah is a biblical figure from the book of [Genesis](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis?tab=contents), described as the sole righteous person among a wicked generation. When God decided to flood the earth, God warned Noah and instructed him to build an ark. During the flood, Noah, his family, and representatives of every animal survived on the ark.

writing.bio: Written texts play a central role in Jewish tradition. The means of the composition and transmission of texts has varied through Jewish history as different technologies have become available — from carved stone tablets and memorized oral traditions to hand-copied manuscripts to printed books to digital resources. Even with adaptations to new technologies, the ancient scribal practice of hand-writing Torah scrolls continues until today, with particular materials and practices required by law and custom. Works such as the [Mishnah](https://www.sefaria.org/texts/Mishnah) and the [Talmud](https://www.sefaria.org/texts/Talmud) are considered to be in the category of [Oral Torah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/oral-torah?tab=notable-sources), even though they have long ago been written down, in

responsibility.bio: From the outset of the Torah, its characters are challenged to take responsibility for their actions: God questions Adam and Eve about their sin in the Garden of Eden and later asks their son Cain about the whereabouts of the brother whom he murdered. Responsibility is a foundation of the covenantal pact between God and the Israelites that appears throughout the Bible. The concept that [all Jews are responsible for one another](https://www.sefaria.org/Sifra%2C_Bechukotai%2C_Chapter_7.5?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) reverberates throughout Jewish texts, as they delve into the practical applications of responsibility and its theoretical underpinnings.

freedom1.bio: Freedom is a concept prevalent throughout Jewish texts. The term is often associated with the biblical story of the Exodus, in which the Israelites were freed from slavery in Egypt, as well as with the ability to perform good deeds and make positive choices.

the-copper-altar.bio: The Copper Altar (Mizbeach Hanechoshet) is the altar upon which sacrifices and libations are offered. The Copper Altar is located within the Temple compound but outside the shrine itself.

zimri.bio: Zimri, a prince of the tribe of Shimon, was killed by Pinchas for his illicit relationship with a Midianite woman, Cozbi, during a plague in Israel.

censuses.bio: The Torah describes several censuses of the people of Israel throughout the books of Exodus and Numbers. These censuses are conducted to collect donations for the Tabernacle, for military preparation, and for tribal organization. Later sources analyze the halakhic ways to do a census, the spiritual significance of these biblical censuses, and more.

workers.bio: The Torah instructs treating laborers justly, forbidding delaying their payment. Various rabbinic sources discuss the value of labor and the honor it gives to one who does it, responsibilities of employers toward their workers, and the rights and responsibilities of workers.

homes.bio: In Jewish tradition, having a home means that one has a place in which to fulfill such obligations as [welcoming guests](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/welcoming-guests?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources), placing [mezuzot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mezuzah?tab=notable-sources) on their doorposts, and commemorating the [destruction of the Temples](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/destruction-of-the-temple?tab=notable-sources). Jewish tradition also uses the idea of home as a metaphor, likening a wife to a home and imagining God as dwelling in God’s homes of the [Tabernacle](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=notable-sources) and, later, the [Temples](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/beit-hamikdash?tab=notable-sources). Peace in one's home ([shalom bayit](https://www.sefaria.or

the-messianic-era.bio: Belief in the coming of the Messiah and the messianic era has been a principle of faith for the Jewish people throughout the Jewish textual tradition. Liturgy includes yearning for the messianic era, and prophets and later commentaries and texts explore what that era will look like. Some describe the era as cosmic in nature with supernatural elements, while others view it as more limited in scope and focus on rational outcomes. In all of these, however, exists a hope for a better future.

ibn-ezra.bio: Avraham ben Meir ibn Ezra, better known simply as Ibn Ezra, was a medieval Spanish Torah commentator, poet, philosopher, and grammarian. A polymath, he wrote on grammar, philosophy, astronomy, medicine, and mathematics. He is most famous for his biblical commentaries, which, alongside those of Rashi, are ubiquitous and indispensable. His commentaries focus on rational, grammatical explanations and the *peshat* (plain sense) meaning of the text. He is often critical of other commentators and especially of Karaites, a group of Jews who rejected the Oral Torah and rabbinic teachings. He maintained a deep friendship with the contemporary Spanish philosopher, [Judah Halevi](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yehuda-halevi?tab=popular-writing-of), and quotes some of his interpretations in his commen

rome.bio: As the nation responsible for the destruction of the Temple in Jerusalem, Rome has a prominent place in the Jewish literature. Although most sources are critical of the pride and viciousness of the Roman Empire, some sources are more apologetic about Rome.

rabban-gamliel-hazaken-(i).bio: Rabban Gamliel became Nasi soon after Hillel died, shortly before the Temple's destruction. Part of his tenure came during a short respite from the Jews' troubles with the reign of Agrippa, who was befriended and taught by R. Gamliel. Many leniencies were legislated under him, and it is was likely he who brought the Great Academy of Jerusalem to Yavneh.

hope.bio: Biblical books are filled with reassurances from God and the prophets that a hopeful future lies ahead. Later Jewish texts offer guidance on how to turn despair into hope, analyze the relationship between hope, trust, and fear, and provide language for expressing hope through prayer.

rabbi-mana-(i).bio: Rabbi Mana (I) was a student of Rabbi Yannai and an influential teacher at the time of R. Yochanan.

music.bio: Music has played a role in Judaism since biblical times: the people of Israel sang and danced as they crossed the Red Sea, David played the harp to calm King Saul, and the book of Psalms consists of 150 poems, some of which describe calling to God [through music](https://www.sefaria.org/Psalms.144.9?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he). Rabbinic literature describes the songs the Levites sang in the Temple and discusses halakhic questions relating to music. Music is a central part of contemporary Jewish life for many, from the tunes associated with liturgical texts, the chanting of the weekly Torah portion, and Jewish songs sung on Shabbat and other occasions.

chametz.bio: Chametz refers to leavened [bread](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/bread?tab=sources), understood by the rabbis to be any food containing one of five species of grain — wheat, barley, oat, spelt and rye — that has been allowed to rise. Several biblical verses command that one abstain from consuming chametz on the Passover holiday and to avoid encountering it in other ways.

nehemiah.bio: Nehemiah was a Jewish courtier in Persia who became one of the leaders of the Jewish return to Judah and a supervisor of the rebuilding of the Temple and renewal of Torah based law.

rabbi-simlai.bio: R. Simlai first moved from Babylonia to Lud, but later moved to the Sanhedrin's seat in the Galilee, where he became an important scholar in Rabbi's court. He is known for his debates with Christians, as well as for his homiletical teachings.

divorce.bio: Though the Torah gives few procedural details, under certain circumstances, it does allow for divorce. The laws of divorce are developed in detail throughout the rabbinic period and in later halakhic literature. To this day, rabbis and Jewish leaders continue to grapple with the intricacies and consequences of these laws. And in that marriage is an oft-used metaphor for the relationship between God and the Jewish people, so too does divorce appear as an oft-rejected possibility.

oils1.bio: Oil features in various contexts in the Jewish textual tradition. In the Bible, oil is used to anoint kings, High Priests, and vessels of the [Tabernacle (mishkan)](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mishkan?tab=sources), as fuel for the [Menorah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/menorah1?tab=sources), and in various biblical stories. Oil also features as an element in food, cosmetics, medicine, and sacrifices, among other functions.

parashat-nitzavim.bio: In Nitzavim (“Standing”), Moses addresses the Israelites, emphasizing the importance of following God’s covenant and of not worshiping other gods. He describes the process of repentance and returning to God, and stresses that God’s commandments are achievable and “not in the heavens.”

zevulun.bio: Zevulun was the tenth son of Jacob and the sixth with his wife Leah. He was the founder of the Tribe of Zevulun.

midian.bio: Located between Israel and Egypt, Midian was inhabited by the descendents of Midian, who was a son of Abraham. Moses spent his exile from Egypt in Midian and married a Midianite woman. Later, Israel was persecuted by Midian on the final legs of their journey to Israel.

nachshon-ben-aminadav.bio: Nachshon ben Aminadav was a biblical figure, who served as a leader of the tribe of Judah. He was the brother of Elisheva, the wife of Aaron the Priest. He is known primarily for an act described in rabbinic literature, in which he was the first to jump into the Red Sea as the people of Israel escaped their enslavement in Egypt, prompting God to split the sea. In the wake of that story, Nachshon is often used as  a symbol of initiative and courage.

welcoming-guests.bio: Welcoming guests — *hachnasat orchim* in Hebrew — is considered an act of loving-kindness and a commandment in Judaism, described as greater than [greeting the divine presence](https://www.sefaria.org/Shabbat.127a.13?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Modeled most prominently by the biblical character [Abraham](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/abraham?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources), welcoming guests can involve housing them, feeding them, and making them feel comfortable.

imagination.bio: Though the contemporary Hebrew word for imagination, *dimyon*, is not found in the Torah, the first book of the Torah speaks of "[thoughts of the heart](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.6.5?lang=bi&with=Translations&lang2=en)," and characters throughout the Bible employ imaginative thinking to [build](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.11.3?lang=bi&with=Translations&lang2=en), [protect](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.12.13?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en), [create](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.31.4?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en), and [develop schemes](https://www.sefaria.org/II_Samuel.14.2?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Later sourc

issachar.bio: Issachar was the ninth son of Jacob and the fifth with his wife Leah. He was the founder of the Tribe of Issachar.

fruit.bio: Fruit (p’ri) plays a central role in Judaism, from the forbidden fruit in the Garden of Eden to the seven species — which includes grapes, figs, and pomegranates — brought as first fruit (bikkurim) offerings to the Temple in Jerusalem.

adam-kadmon1.bio: *Adam Kadmon* is the first and innermost world, which precedes the four worlds of [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut) ([Emanation](/topics/atzilut)), [*Beria*](/topics/beria1) ([Creation](/topics/beria1)), [*Yetzira*](/topics/yetzira1) ([Formation](/topics/yetzira1)), and [*Asiya*](/topics/asiya2) ([Action](/topics/asiya2)). It incorporates all of existence, from its head, that is, the source, which is [*Ein Sof*](/topics/ein-sof1), to its lowest point, which is this physical world. This world is called *Adam Kadmon* because it is depicted in kabbalistic writings in the form of a man, having a head, body and appendages. Although all the worlds are likewise described in the form of a man, this world is the primeval one, before which there is no world, while the other worlds, which are external to

maturity2.bio: The [brains](/topics/brains2) of [*Abba*](/topics/abba1), that is, the illumination of [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), are called maturity (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 7, Q & A 31).

jubilee.bio: The jubilee (yovel) year is the 50th year in the biblical agricultural cycle and occurs after seven cycles of seven-year shemittah cycles. During the jubilee year, [a shofar is blown on Yom Kippur](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.25.9?ven=The_Contemporary_Torah,_Jewish_Publication_Society,_2006&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he), land ownership resets to its original holders, and all Israelite slaves are set free. Like the [shemittah (sabbatical year)](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shemitah?tab=sources) year, the jubilee year is one in which the land is not worked.

mezuzah.bio: A mezuzah is the small parchment placed inside a case and attached to the right side of entryway doorframes where Jews reside. Written on the scroll are various sections from the Torah, such as the [Shema](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shema?tab=sources), that feature some of Judaism's central principles.

asiya1.bio: The lowest of the four worlds (planes, or [levels](/topics/level) of reality), the world of *Asiya* parallels the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1). Accordingly, it is also correlated with the “fourth [level](/topics/level)” of a given structure of reality, like [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), which is the fourth [level](/topics/level) of the five main [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) ([*Keter*](/topics/keter1) is the root [level](/topics/level), followed by the next four, hence, the final [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) is considered “fourth,” as is the plane of *Asiya*). It is called *Asiya*, or “Actualization,” because it is where all the wills and forces that descend from the worlds above it are actualized (*na’asim*), or materialized,

shemini-atzeret.bio: Shemini Atzeret is an early fall holiday that marks the end of the harvest and the start of the rainy season. It directly follows the holiday of [Sukkot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/sukkot?sort=Relevance&tab=sources) and is observed with prayers for rain in Israel.

hagar.bio: When Sarah struggled with infertility, she gave Abraham her servant, Hagar, to be his concubine. She bore him Ishmael and was afterwards expelled to the desert with her son. They were rescued by an angel and became the ancestors of the Ishmaelites.

parashat-haazinu.bio: In Ha’azinu (“Listen”) , Moses recites a poem praising God and criticizing the sins of the Israelites. He describes the misfortunes that the Israelites will face and the damage God will ultimately wreak on their oppressors. The portion ends as God commands Moses to ascend Mount Nebo, where he is to die.

destruction-of-the-temple.bio: The destruction of the Temple can refer to the destruction of either of the two Temples that stood in Jerusalem in the ancient period. The First Temple, built by King Solomon, as described in the book of I Kings, was destroyed in 586 BCE by Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon. The Second Temple, built approximately 70 years after the destruction of the first, was destroyed in 70 CE by the Roman Empire.

parashat-matot.bio: Matot (“Tribes”) opens with laws about vows, and continues to describe the Israelites’ war against the Midianites and the allocation of spoils. The tribes of Reuben and Gad request to dwell outside of the Land of Israel, and Moses acquiesces on the condition that they help conquer it.

challah.bio: Although challah today is simply known as the braided loaf of bread eaten on Shabbat and Jewish holidays, its origins are in the biblical commandment to separate a portion of dough — known as challah — that would then be given to the priest (kohen).

lulav.bio: The Lulav is a closed palm frond shaken on Sukkot alongside willow (arava) and myrtle (hadas) branches, as well as with a citron (etrog). Although the word Lulav technically only means “palm branch,” it’s usually used to refer to all of the branches and sometimes the citron as well.

song-of-songs.bio: [Song of Songs](https://www.sefaria.org/Song_of_Songs?tab=contents) (“Shir Hashirim”) is one of the five megillot (scrolls), part of the section of the Hebrew Bible called Writings. Attributed in its opening verse to [King Solomon](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/king-solomon?tab=sources), the book records poetic conversations between two lovers, describing their pursuit of each other through vineyards and fields of blossoming flowers. It is traditionally understood as a metaphor for the loving relationship between God and the people of Israel, and is often read publicly on Passover or on Shabbat evening.

murder.bio: Murder is one of the three most grievous sins in Judaism for which the rabbis teach that one is to let oneself be killed rather than kill another. Jewish texts discuss this law and also speak about the spiritual and social implications of taking another's life and also the very limited circumstances under which it is permissible to kill another person.

distinctions-of-the-land-of-israel.canonical: Distinctiveness of the Land of Israel
aliases: Distinctiveness of the Land of Israel
distinctions-of-the-land-of-israel.bio: In the Torah, the land of Israel  is described as rife with produce and resources, a land where following God’s commandments is rewarded and flaunting them can cause expulsion. Unlike Egypt, biblical Israel was a land almost entirely dependent on rain, and the Israelites would be more dependent on God when living there. Poets and scholars have long yearned to be in the land of Israel, and its merits and holiness have been extolled across many genres.

naftali.bio: Naftali was the sixth son of Jacob and the second with his concubine Bilhah. He is the eponymous ancestor of the tribe of Naftali.

sitra-achra.bio: Sitra Achra (literally "other side") is a kabbalistic term refering to the realm of evil or impure forces, in opposition to the Sitra D’Kedushah, the side of holiness.

rabbi-tanhuma-b-abba.bio: R. Tanhuma b. Abba seems to have been a student of Rav Huna b. Avin, as he brings down several of his teachings. He was known for his piety and is noted to have had at least one interview with the Roman emperor.

social-justice.bio: Through the Torah's commandments and the prophets' voices, God demands that the Jewish people behave with justice and righteousness. These imperatives are taken up by the ancient rabbis and later Jewish thinkers who endeavor to further define what justice is, what it requires of us, and to imagine what a just world might look like, or if that will only come to be in a messianic age.

unity.bio: Jewish tradition, with roots in midrash, maintains that Israelites stood at [Mount Sinai](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/mount-sinai?tab=admin) as though they were “one person with one heart.” [Acheinu](https://www.sefaria.org/Siddur_Ashkenaz%2C_Weekday%2C_Shacharit%2C_Torah_Reading%2C_Reading_from_Sefer%2C_Raising_the_Torah.9?ven=hebrew|The_Metsudah_siddur,_1981&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en), a prayer expressing Jewish unity, occupies an important place for many in Jewish prayer. The Torah also warns of the dangers of uniformity, through stories like the [Tower of Babel](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tower-of-babel?tab=admin), and Jewish texts throughout the ages consider how to balance unity with values that at times conflict with it.

parashat-devarim.bio: Devarim (“Words”) is the first Torah portion in the Book of Deuteronomy, the final book of the Torah. In it, Moses recounts events from the Israelites’ travels in the desert, like the appointment of judges, the sin of the spies, and the wars with the Emorite kings Sihon and Og.

vashti.bio: Vashti is the wife of Ahashverosh and appears in the first chapter of the [book of Esther](https://www.sefaria.org/Esther?tab=contents). Vashti refuses to come when Ahashverosh calls for her to display her beauty to his male friends at a banquet.

natan.bio: Natan HaNavi, or Nathan the prophet, was a prophet during the time periods of King David and King Solomon. His actions are described in the books of Samuel, Kings, and Chronicles.

cities-of-refuge.bio: In the books of [Numbers](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.35.9?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) and [Deuteronomy](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.19.2?lang=bi&aliyot=0), the Torah discusses the establishment of six cities of refuge in the land of Israel to which people who killed accidentally would flee, thereby avoiding being killed by family members of the victim. These cities were part of a network of cities for the tribe of Levi. Cities of refuge and the laws surrounding them are the subject of the second chapter of tractate Makkot in the [Mishnah](https://www.sefaria.org/Mishnah_Makkot?tab=contents) and [Talmud](https://www.sefaria.org/Makkot?tab=contents).

vidui.bio: Viddui is confession of sins. It is said at certain times, such as Yom Kippur and right before one's death. There are different lists of sins that are generally recited — the longest of which, by Rabbeinu Nissim of Bavel, includes hundreds of sins — but it can also be improvised.

abel.bio: Abel was second son of Adam and Eve and the first shepherd. After God accepted his offerings of choice animals but rejected his brother Cain's offerings of fruits and vegetables, he was killed by his jealous brother.

rabbi-abba-b-r-hiyya-b-abba.canonical: Rabbi Abba b. R. Hiyya [b. Abba]
aliases: Rabbi Abba b. R. Hiyya [b. Abba]
Rabbi Abba b. Hiyya b. Abba
Rabbi Abba ben R. Hiyya [b. Abba]
Rabbi Abba ben Hiyya ben Abba
Rebbi Abba b. Rebbi Hiyya b. Abba
Rabbi Abba b. Rabbi Hiyya b. Abba
rabbi-abba-b-r-hiyya-b-abba.bio: Rabbi Abba b. R. Hiyya [b. Abba] was the son of R. Hiyya b. Abba who was R. Yochanan's student. R. Abba also brings down teachings from R. Yochanan which R, Abba presumably got from his father.

kush.bio: In the Torah, Kush was Noah's grandson, the son of Noah's son Ham. As Noah's descendants repopulated the world after the flood, Kush became an ancient kingdom in the southern Nile Valley, often identified with the region now known as Ethiopia in Jewish sources. The term also becomes the adjective used in rabbinic texts to refer to Black people. It has become a slur in modern Hebrew.

daughters-of-zelophehad.bio: The daughters of Tzelofchad were the only children of the biblical character Tzelofchad. As only men were allowed to inherit their fathers' land, the daughters were concerned that Tzelofchad's land would leave their family. They petitioned Moses, and God responded that if a man had no sons, daughters could inherit his land.

yokheved.bio: Yokheved is a figure in the book of Exodus and the mother of [Aaron](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/aaron), [Miriam](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/miriam), and Moses. Fearing Pharaoh’s decree to throw all Israelite baby boys into the river, she hides her infant son, [Moses](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/moses). When that is no longer feasible, she places him in a basket on the Nile river, where he would be found by a maidservant to [Pharaoh's daughter](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/pharaohs-daughter).

shacharit.bio: The morning prayer service in Judaism—*Shacharit* in Hebrew—consists of four main sections: preparatory prayers and readings; the [Shema](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shema?tab=admin) and its blessings; the [Amidah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shemoneh-esrei?sort=Relevance&tab=admin); and the concluding sections. The Torah is read on Mondays, Thursdays, and Shabbat, as well as on most other special days. Rabbinic sources delve into the origins and significance of daily morning prayers.

serah-the-daughter-of-asher.bio: Serah is the daughter of [Asher](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/asher?sort=Relevance&tab=sources), son of [Jacob](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/jacob?tab=sources). Rabbinic tradition suggests that she never died and has played a few significant roles throughout Jewish history, including telling Jacob the shocking news that Joseph was alive in Egypt and identifying Moses and Aaron as the redeemers of Israel from slavery.

shalosh-regalim.bio: The *shalosh regalim* are the three pilgrimage festivals discussed in the Torah: Passover, [Shavuot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shavuot?sort=Relevance&tab=sources), and [Sukkot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/sukkot?tab=sources). When the Temple in Jerusalem existed,  people would celebrate these festivals in Jerusalem.

the-four-species.bio: The four species are four plants associated with the holiday of Sukkot. The commandment to gather the four species originates in Leviticus 23:40. Rabbinic sources discuss the exact meaning of each of the phrases in the verse and ultimately identify the four species as: the etrog (citron fruit), lulav (closed frond of a palm tree), hadas (myrtle bough) and aravah (willow branch).

hunger.bio: From the outset of the Bible, biblical figures experience hunger and famine: Abraham and Sarah [leave their home](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.12.10?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) in the wake of a famine, for example, and the people of Israel [experience hunger](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.8.3?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) in the desert before God provides them with manna. Later Jewish texts describe the importance of recognizing hunger experienced by other people and discuss the obligation of charitably feeding the hungry.

children.bio: Numerous Jewish sources highlight the importance of bringing children into the world and nurturing their growth. Children are viewed as both a blessing and a vital link in the continuity of the Jewish people. The commandment “Be fruitful and multiply,” one of the first mitzvot in the Torah, establishes the obligation to have children. The rabbinic sages regarded teaching Torah to children as a supreme value, teaching that the world’s very future depends on the pure words spoken by young children learning Torah. Many Jewish texts describe raising children as a process of passing on values, mitzvot, and character through loving guidance and example.

thanks.bio: Gratitude, or the concept of being thankful, is prevalent throughout Jewish texts. It is the theme of the first prayer uttered upon waking up in the morning, Modeh Ani (“I give thanks”), and is a value that drives countless other Jewish practices, traditions, and customs.

sag.bio: *Adam Kadmon* is the first and innermost world, which precedes the four worlds of [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut) ([Emanation](/topics/atzilut)), [*Beria*](/topics/beria1) ([Creation](/topics/beria1)), [*Yetzira*](/topics/yetzira1) ([Formation](/topics/yetzira1)), and [*Asiya*](/topics/asiya2) ([Action](/topics/asiya2)). It incorporates all of existence, from its head, that is, the source, which is [*Ein Sof*](/topics/ein-sof1), to its lowest point, which is this physical world. This world is called *Adam Kadmon* because it is depicted in kabbalistic writings in the form of a man, having a head, body and appendages. Although all the worlds are likewise described in the form of a man, this world is the primeval one, before which there is no world, while the other worlds, which are external to

strength.bio: Strength is a trait and value discussed at length in Jewish sources. Biblical stories feature physically strong heroes, such as Samson in the book of Judges. Rabbinic texts  analyze the merits of different types of strength and how they should be valued and prioritized.

middot.bio: Middot literally means “measures,” but it’s typically translated as “character traits,” because a person is defined—or measured—by their character traits.

kiddush.bio: Kiddush — literally, sanctification — is the ceremonial prayer before the main meals on Shabbat and holidays, recognizing and sanctifying the holy day. It generally includes a blessing on wine and at least one paragraph about the holiness of the day.

aliases: Rabbi Yosei b. Yo'ezer of Tzereida
Yose b. Yoezer (a man) of Zeredah
Rabbi Yose b. Yoezer, a man of Zereda
Rabbi Yosei ben Yo'ezer of Tzereida
Yose ben Yoezer (a man) of Zeredah
Rabbi Yose ben Yoezer, a man of Zereda
yose-b-yoezer.bio: Yose b. Yoezer and Yose b. Yochanan served as the first of the zugot (pairs), with the first being Nasi (religious leader) and the second the head of the court. They served after Antigonos in a period of intense Hellenization and war, and promulgated several leniencies to encourage Jews to keep the law under these difficult conditions.

the-four-kingdoms.bio: The Four Kingdoms refers to the four empires — Babylon, Persia, Greece, and Rome — that ruled over the Land of Israel and resulted in the exile of the Jewish people. These four empires and exiles are represented by different creatures in a prophetic vision in the Book of Daniel.

nahum-hamedi.bio: Even though Nahum haMedi was known as one of the outstanding early judges in Jerusalem, his colleagues generally disagreed with him.

adam-and-eve.bio: [Adam](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/adam?tab=sources) and [Eve](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/eve?tab=sources) feature in the beginning of the book of [Genesis](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis?tab=contents). They were the first people, and the ancestors of all humanity.

visiting-the-sick.bio: In Judaism, visiting the sick (bikur cholim) is an important act of loving-kindness (gemilut hasadim) that requires one to provide physical or emotional support to someone who isn't feeling well.

joseph-karo.bio: Joseph Karo, expelled from Spain as a child, was a rabbi, talmudist, mystic, and preemiment halakhic codifier. His best-known work, the Shulchan Arukh, was accepted in his lifetime and formally recognized thereafter as the definitive statement of Jewish legal and religious practice. He also wrote basic commentaries on Rambam's Mishneh Torah and Jacob ben Ashe's Arba'ah Turim, both of which were major sources for his own Shulchan Arukh. As a mystic, he also received heavenly revelations. Some of these were set down in writing and contributed to his decision to migrate to the land of Israel. There, he lived in Safed, where he supported the effort of his teacher, Rabbi Yaakov Berav, to revive traditional rabbinic ordination (Hebrew: semikhah).

maaser-sheni.bio: Ma'aser Sheni (Second Tithe) is a second tithe taken from produce in Israel during the first, second, fourth, and fifth years of the seven-year cycle. As opposed to the other tithes, it is kept by the owner on condition that he eats it in Jerusalem.

gods-existence.bio: Many Jewish texts presuppose the existence of God, while others seek  to prove God’s existence through rational arguments, citation of miracles, or personal experience.

praise-of-god.bio: Praise of God involves expressing gratitude and admiration directly to Him in diverse situations and contexts. While the book of Psalms contains numerous instances of such praise, similar expressions can also be found in other books of the Bible and subsequent religious texts.

love-of-god1.bio: In a passage recited daily as part of the [Shema](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shema?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) prayer, the Torah commands loving God “with all your heart, soul, and might.” For thousands of years. Jewish commentators, thinkers, and legal scholars have explored the meaning of this commandment, how it can be accomplished, and its role in Judaism.

jonathan1.bio: Jonathan was the son of King Saul and a beloved companion of David's. He took David's side over his father's and saved David's life. Jonathan was eventually killed in battle alongside his father and brothers.

omer-offering.bio: The Omer Offering (Korban HaOmer) consisted of a measure of barley that was brought to the Temple on the second night of Passover, which coincides with the barley harvest in Israel. Then, 49 days are counted until the wheat harvest, which is known as “Counting the Omer.”

nefesh.bio: This is the name for the first and lowest of the five [levels](/topics/level) (and corresponding lights) of the soul, *nefesh*, [*ruaḥ*](/topics/ruah1), [*neshama*](/topics/neshama2), [*ḥaya*](/topics/haya1), and [*yeḥida*](/topics/yehida1). These five [levels](/topics/level) of the soul correspond to the five primary *sefirot*, of [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), as well as the five worlds, [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2), [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut), [*Beria*](/topics/beria1), [*Yetzira*](/topics/yetzira1), and [*Asiya*](/topics/asiya2). The concept of the five [levels](/topics/level) of the soul relates to five qualities of supernal light which illuminate people as we

aram.bio: Aram was a son of Shem and the ancestor of the Arameans, the people from whom Abraham descended, as well as Rebecca and her brother Laban. In Jewish literature, Laban is often known as the Aramean.

second-constriction.bio: The second constriction occurred in one of the formative stages of the [*partzufim*](/topics/partzuf) of [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2) , in order to enable the creation of man and this world. The fourth [level](/topics/level), or phase of creation, which constitutes the will to receive, or the attribute of judgment, also known as the [level](/topics/level) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), rose and mingled with the second [level](/topics/level), which constitutes the will to give, or the attribute of mercy, also known as the [level](/topics/level) of [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), and thus the [partition](/topics/partition2) that is upon the [level](/topics/level) [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) rose to the second [level](/topics/level) as well. As a consequence of the [partition](/topics/

ketubah.bio: A ketubah is a Jewish marriage contract, outlining the rights and responsibilities between spouses. It is an integral part of a traditional Jewish marriage.

shimon-hatimni.bio: Shimon HaTimni was a scholar that lived during the time of R. Akiva and was a colleague of R. Yehudah b. Baba.

aliases: Moses at The Waters of Contention
the-waters-of-meribah.bio: Meribah is a place mentioned in the books of Exodus and Numbers. In one story in Exodus, the Israelites complain about a lack of water. God instructs Moses to hit a rock; Moses does, and water comes out. The place is then called Massah and Meribah, commemorating the incident. In a similar story described in Numbers, God instructs Moses to take his staff and talk to a rock after the Israelites complain about a lack of water. Moses hits the rock twice and water comes out. God then tells Moses and Aaron that they will not enter Israel, and refers to the water as "water of Meribah."

kabbalat-shabbat.bio: Kabbalat Shabbat (Welcoming the Shabbat) is a service developed by the Kabbalists of Safed in the 16th century. It is composed primarily of Psalms, but includes other elements. These include the famous hymn, Lecha Dodi (Come, my beloved), which was written there at that time.

hate.bio: Hatred is a concept that appears throughout the Jewish textual tradition. The Torah commands against harboring hatred for one’s fellow in one’s heart, and rabbinic sources discuss the parameters of hatred, under what circumstances hatred might be permitted or even encouraged, how to avoid harboring hatred, and the halakhic ramifications for one whose actions are motivated by hate.

deborah.bio: Deborah was a prophetess in the book of Judges who judged the Israelites from under her palm tree. She advised Barak on his military campaign against the Assyrian commander Sisera.

the-book-of-ruth.bio: The [book of Ruth](https://www.sefaria.org/Ruth.1?lang=bi) is one of the five megillot (scrolls) found in the section of the Hebrew Bible called Writings and is traditionally read on the holiday of Shavuot. It tells the story of [Ruth](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/ruth?tab=sources), a widow of Moabite origin, who insists on staying with her widowed, Israelite mother-in-law, declaring “Wherever you go, I will go... your people shall be my people, and your God my God” (1:16). Ruth’s loyalty leads her to the field of her kinsman, Boaz, whom she ultimately marries. Together they have a child, who later becomes the grandfather of King David.

laws-of-annulling-vows.bio: Hatarat Nedarim (the annulment of vows) is a legal process which allows one to cancel a vow by reciting a text in the presence of three people. It is customary to perform hatarat nedarim in the days leading up to [Rosh HaShanah](https://www.sefaria.org/Rosh_Hashanah?tab=contents) or [Yom Kippur](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yom-kippur?sort=Relevance&tab=sources), in a version of the ceremony that involves cancelling all vows taken in the past and to be taken in the future.

parashat-vayigash.bio: Vayiggash (“He Approached”) opens as Judah pleads with Joseph not to keep Benjamin as a prisoner. Joseph reveals his true identity to his brothers, crying and kissing them. The brothers bring Jacob from Canaan to Egypt, and Jacob and his children settle in Goshen. The portion ends as Joseph buys most of Egypt’s land in exchange for food.

tikkun.bio: *Tikkun* means “repair” or “mend.” It is used alongside the word *olam* (world) to express the Jewish responsibility to [repair the world](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/tikkun-olam?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources). *Tikkun* can also refer to specific texts read on various occasions, specific nights of Torah study, such as [Hoshanah Rabbah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/hoshana-rabbah?tab=admin) and [Shavuot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shavuot?tab=admin), the process of making a shared courtyard into a personal space so that one can carry in it on Shabbat, or the process of improving [character traits](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/middot?tab=admin).

gideon.bio: Gideon was a judge and military leader in the Book of Judges. He led the under-dog Israelites to victory against the Midianites. He was offered kingship, but refused it, declaring that God is the only sovereign.

yefet.bio: Yefet, known as Japheth in English, is a biblical character first mentioned in Genesis 6:10, where he is listed as the third of Noah’s three sons. Together with his brother Shem, he covers his father’s nakedness when Noah gets drunk after emerging from the ark. For this, Noah blesses him. He is described as having seven sons, among them Yavan, considered to be the father of the Greeks.

nachman-of-breslov.bio: Rebbe Nachman of Breslov was one of the most creative Chasidic masters, whose thought and teachings continue to resonate far beyond his immediate followers. He was a great-grandson of the founder of Chasidut, [Rabbi Yisrael Ba'al Shem Tov](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/baal-shem-tov?tab=popular-writing-of), and a grandson of another early Chasidic master, Rabbi Nachman of Horodenka. Among his many works are a profound presentation of his Chasidic and mystical teaching, Likkutei Moharan, and a compilation of his intricate and powerfully evocative stories, Sippurei Ma'asiyot. Another work relates the events of his transformative journey to and from the land of Israel. He was severely criticized by certain contemporary Chasidic masters but was defended by the leading Chasidic master, [Rabbi

aarons-sin.bio: Exodus 32 describes the moment when the people of Israel abandon hope that Moses will return from Mount Sinai and turn to Aaron to forge a golden calf to worship. Commentaries across genres discuss the responsibility Aaron bears for this paradigmatic sin, delving into his motivations and deliberations. In many of these commentaries, Aaron's later role as high priest is connected to this moment, as are the specifications for the sin offering.

parashat-vayeilech.bio: Vayelekh (“He Went”) opens as Moses tells the people of Israel that he will not lead them into the land of Israel and that Joshua will take over. He instructs the people to gather and read Torah publicly every seven years. At God’s command, Moses writes a poem bearing witness to God’s covenant with the people of Israel.

sadness.bio: Sadness is a central part of the human experience. Biblical narratives include sadness as motivating biblical figures — even God. Halakhic texts limit when it is acceptable to be sad and dictate when one must be. Other texts include psychological insights, self-help advice,  and prayers and meditations for help to overcome the pain.

gog-and-magog.bio: “Gog and Magog” refers to enemies of the Jewish people. Jewish texts describe how, at the dawn of a messianic era, Gog and Magog will wage war against Israel, and God will miraculously defeat them. The terms Gog and Magog first appear together in [Ezekiel 38–39](https://www.sefaria.org/Ezekiel.38.2?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he), where Gog is the name of a leader, and Magog is his land. In rabbinic literature, Gog and Magog are understood to be the names of two nations or one nation with two names.

shefa1.bio: Often translated as bounty, flow, or abundance, *shefa* comes from the Hebrew word *hashpa’a*, which in this context means to give. Rays of sunlight, for example, can be described as the sun’s *shefa*. The rays constitute a “flow” of light from the sun that provides light and sustenance to the earth in bountiful abundance. In kabbalistic thought, *shefa*  refers to different forms of light that flow from God's essence or from the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) and the like. Additionally, *shefa* can be used to refer both to God’s giving to creation, as well as the giving of created beings to each other.

shmuel-the-little.bio: Shmuel the Little was a scholar known for his great humility and concern for others. Hence he was chosen to compose the text of a blessing (in the Amidah prayer) calling for the elimination of those endangering the Jewish people.

beria1.bio: The bringing forth of something into existence ex nihilo, [*yesh* *me*’*ayin*](/topics/yesh-meayin), is known as *Beria* (Creation). Since the [opacity](/topics/opacity) ([*ovyut*](/topics/ovyut)) in the vessels cannot be revealed in the world of [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut) ([Emanation](/topics/atzilut)), but only from the ground of the world of [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut) ([Emanation](/topics/atzilut)) and below, it is therefore called *Beria* from that point and below, after the [opacity](/topics/opacity) ([*ovyut*](/topics/ovyut)) that has been revealed. For the [opacity](/topics/opacity) ([*ovyut*](/topics/ovyut)), which is comprised of the [will to receive](/topics/will-to-receive) in the created being, is a form of [*yesh* *me*’*ayin*](/topics/yesh-meayin), creation from nothing. E

temple-vessels.bio: The Temple Vessels (Kli Hamikdash) are the main furnishings of the Temple and include the Menorah, the table, the Ark and the two altars.

shimon-b-chalafta.bio: Shimon b. Chalafta came from an illustrious family and was a regular at R. Yehudah haNasi's academy. There he developed a close friendship with R. Yehudah's son, Shimon, and with the academy's leading student, R. Chiyya. In spite of his lineage, he subsisted on little until helped by R. Chiyya.

micah.bio: Micah son of Imlah, also known as Micaiah or Mikhah in Hebrew, prophesied the death of King Ahab of the Northern Kingdom of Israel.

pharaohs-daughter.bio: There are two women referred to in Tanakh as "daughter of Pharaoh." The first is the woman who pulled [Moses](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/moses) from the Nile and raised him as her son, in her father's house. According to rabbinic tradition, she converted to Judaism, and her name was Bityah, meaning "daughter of God." The second is a wife of [King Solomon](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/king-solomon).

parashat-masei.bio: Masei (“Travels”), the final Torah portion in the book of Numbers, opens with a list of places that the Israelites traveled in the desert. God commands the Israelites to destroy idolatry in the Land of Israel, outlines Israel’s boundaries, and details the laws of cities of refuge for accidental killers.

levirate-marriage.bio: *Yibbum* is the Torah-mandated marriage of a widow to the brother of her childless husband. If either party does not want to go through with the marriage, an alternative ceremony called [*chalitzah*](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/halitzah?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) is performed. In most contemporary Jewish communities, *chalitzah* is practiced instead of *yibbum*.

calendar.bio: The Jewish calendar is a lunarsolar calendar, incorporating elements of a lunar and solar calendar. It consists of 12 months of 29 or 30 days that begin around the time of the new moon, with an extra lunar month added every two or three years. Sources throughout the Jewish textual tradition discuss the development, significance, and legal aspects of the calendar.

song-of-the-sea1.bio: The Song of the Sea, recorded in [Exodus 15:1–18](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.15?lang=bi&aliyot=0), is a song that the Israelites sang upon miraculously crossing the Red Sea. It thanks God for the redemption from slavery in Egypt and celebrates the downfall of the Egyptian oppressors. The song features prominently in Jewish liturgy as part of the daily shacharit (morning) services.

abortion.bio: Though the Bible does not explicitly address the topic of abortion, several biblical texts serve as a basis for Jewish discussions about abortion, most prominent among them [Exodus 21:22–23](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.21.22?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en). Subsequent rabbinic texts, interpretations, responsa, and works of Jewish thought take up the questions of the circumstances in which abortions are permitted or required and weigh the complexities of the many competing factors that require consideration.

world-of-nekudim.bio: The world of *nekudim* is the second of the worlds of [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2). It is called by this name because the spiritual [levels](/topics/level) within it are called *nekudot*, or points. In this context, the *nekuda* refers to [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), the fourth [level](/topics/level), after the first [constriction](/topics/constriction) ([*tzimtzum*](/topics/tzimtzum1)) occurred. As a result of this constriction, [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) is only a black point without any light whatsoever.Then,as a result of the [second constriction](/topics/second-constriction) ([*tzimtzum*](/topics/tzimtzum1)), [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) and the [partition](/topics/partition2) that is upon it became intermingled within all the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) and are then p

revenge.bio: The Torah [prohibits](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.19.18?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) taking personal revenge, but also instructs taking revenge against the [Midianites](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.31.2?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) and notes that [vengeance belongs to God](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.32.35?lang=bi&with=Rashi&lang2=en). The biblical concept of cities of refuge is designed to protect those who killed accidentally from blood avengers, but the instinct of a [blood avenger](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.35.27?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) seems to be recognized as natural. In discussing these cases and others, biblical commentators, rabbinic texts, and ethical books throughout the ages grap

babylonian-exile.bio: The Babylonian Exile (Galut Bavel) began in 586 BCE after Jerusalem and the Holy Temple were destroyed by King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon. Fifty years later, under Persian King Cyrus the Great, the exiles were allowed to return to Jerusalem to rebuild the Temple.

rabban-gamliel.bio: Rabban Gamliel (II) was a tannaitic sage in the first and second centuries CE. He was the first to serve as *nasi* (leader of the *[Sanhedrin](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/sanhedrin?tab=admin)*) after the destruction of the Second Temple and worked to strengthen the Jewish community and leadership in the post-Temple era. Rabban Gamliel occasionally acted in ways that involved publicly shaming other rabbis, which led to pushback and his temporary deposition.

rabbi-yose-b-kippar.bio: R. Yose b. Kippar was a student of R. Elazar b. Shamu'a and was an emissary from the Land of Israel to the ascendant Jewish community in Babylonia.

rabbi-elazar-b-arakh.bio: R. Elazar b. Arakh was one of R. Yochanan b. Zakkai's closest students, and was known for his effective counsel. Yet because he did not join the sages in Yavneh, he forget most of his learning, regaining it only as a result of the prayers of his colleagues.

outside-of-israel.bio: In Judaism, the language “outside of Israel” (chutz laaretz) is more than just a geographical construct — it’s also a legal and theological one. There are different laws for Jews living inside versus those living outside of the land of Israel.

kehillah.bio: A *kehillah* is a community of individuals who come together for a common goal or purpose. Although a kehillah can become a chaotic mass—such as during the sin of the [golden calf](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/golden-calf?tab=admin)—when positive character traits are combined in pursuit of a goal, much good can be achieved.

joseph-and-potiphars-wife.bio: The story of Joseph and Potiphar's wife, in the book of Genesis, recounts a challenging encounter for Joseph. The story lends itself to further interpretation, and texts from various genres have contributed different motivations for both Joseph and Potiphar's wife.

human-dignity.bio: The opening chapter of the Torah describes how God created humanity [in the image of God](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.1.27?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he), a concept that fuels the idea that people are to be treated with dignity and respect. The value of human dignity stands behind many Jewish commandments and customs, such as the obligation to [return a cloak](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.24.13?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) taken as collateral from a poor person as the night approaches or the duty to promptly [bury the dead](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.21.23?ven=english|The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en).

elisha-ben-abuya.bio: Elisha ben Abuyah was a tannaitic sage who adopted a heretical worldview. He is mentioned twice in tannaitic literature and appears in several stories throughout the Talmud and midrashic literature. Elisha is often referred to as “acher” (“other one”) as a result of his heresy.

ham.bio: Ham was a son of Noah who was cursed for looking at the nakedness of his father when Noah got drunk. He was the ancestor of, among others, the Canaanite people.

destruction-of-the-first-temple.bio: The destruction of the Temple can refer to the destruction of either of the two Temples that stood in Jerusalem in the ancient period. The First Temple, built by King Solomon, was destroyed in 586 BCE by Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon. The Second Temple, built 70 years after the destruction of the first, was destroyed in 70 CE by the Roman empire. The fast day of Tisha B'Av commemorates the destruction of both Temples, among other historical tragedies.

aliases: Bezalel b. Uri b. Hur, of the tribe of Judah
betzalel.bio: Betzalel is a biblical character who features in the book of Exodus.
