har-gerizim-vehar-eival.bio: Mount Gerizim and Mount Ebal (Har Gerizim Vehar Eival) are two mountains in Samaria near the Jordan River. The former was fertile and the latter barren, which made them the ideal setting for announcing the blessings and curses in the Bible.

nineveh.bio: Nineveh was the populous capital city of Assyria located in the north of present-day Iraq. In the Bible, it is most famous for the repentance of its residents after God forces Jonah to rebuke them.

mourners-kaddish.bio: Mourner's Kaddish is the practice of mourners to recite Kaddish at the beginning and end of prayer services and other occasions. This declaration of their faith in God, is thought to help the souls of the dead to whom they dedicate their recitation.

haya.bio: *Ḥaya* is the name of the fourth of the five [levels](/topics/level) of the soul: [*nefesh*](/topics/nefesh), [*ruaḥ*](/topics/ruah1), [*neshama*](/topics/neshama2), [*ḥaya*](/topics/haya1), and [*yeḥida*](/topics/yehida1). These five [levels](/topics/level) of the soul correspond to the five primary *sefirot*: [*Keter*](/topics/keter1), [*Ḥokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), [*Bina*](/topics/bina3), [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1). They also correspond to the five worlds: [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2), [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut), [*Beria*](/topics/beria1), [*Yetzira*](/topics/yetzira1), and [*Asiya*](/topics/asiya2). The concept of the five [levels](/topics/level) of the soul relates to five qualities of supernal light which illuminate people as well as th

sadducees.bio: The Sadducees were an influential party that questioned the validity of the Pharisees' oral traditions, including such fundamental beliefs as the survival of the soul after death. Doing away with many of the laws that separated Jews from Greeks, they were able to ingratiate themselves with the wealthy powerful class, such that many political leaders and priests were members of this party. Nevertheless, they eventually failed in their struggle to redefine Judaism.

hoshana-rabbah.bio: Hoshana Rabbah is the seventh day of the holiday [Sukkot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/sukkot?sort=Relevance&tab=sources). In Temple times, the day was marked by seven processions around the [altar](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-copper-altar?tab=sources), which was decorated with willow branches. Today, the day is marked by customs such as seven processions around the synagogue sanctuary, extended recitation of prayers known as *hoshanot,* the striking of willow branches on the floor, and staying up at night to learn Torah.

four-questions.bio: The “Four questions” is a text that features prominently at the Passover seder, opening up the portion of the evening in which the exodus story is discussed. Also referred to as “Ma Nishtanah” (“why is this night different”) after its two opening words, the questions highlight the differences between the seder night and all other nights. The text’s origins are in the Mishnah, with variant versions in different rabbinic texts and haggadahs. It is commonly recited or sung by the youngest person at the seder.

science.bio: Jewish sources throughout the ages grapple with questions of science and the surrounding world, and how these questions intersect with Jewish texts, practices, and values. Some texts view Jewish tradition and science as incompatible, arguing that only one can be a source of truth and the other must be rejected. Others see science and Torah as harmonious and strive to read traditional Jewish sources in conversation with scientific knowledge.

mishloach-manot.bio: The Hebrew term "mishloach manot" literally means "a delivery of portions," and sending them is one of the four mitzvot (commandments) of the holiday of [Purim](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/purim?sort=Relevance&tab=sources). One sends portions of choice foods to their friends on Purim, often in the form of gift baskets or bags of goodies, to contribute to the Purim seudah, or feast, another of the four mitzvot of the day.

marah.bio: Marah, meaning bitter, was a place in the Sinai that the Israelites reached in their wandering. They couldn't drink the water there because it was bitter until Moses performed a miracle to sweeten it.

spices.bio: Although the Bible has no specific word for “spices,” the Talmud uses the term tavlin for these appetite-stimulating and food-improving additions, which were also used in the Temple service for the incense offering.

bar-kokhba.bio: Simon bar Kokhba was a Jewish military leader in Judea. He is known for leading a revolt against the Roman empire, initiated in 132 CE. The rebellion ultimately was quashed; Bar Kokhba was killed by Roman forces, and his followers were either killed or enslaved.

the-worship-of-molech.bio: The Torah singles out the worship of Molekh as particularly abhorrent to God. While the Torah verses clearly state that it involves giving one's child to the deity in a forbidden ritual, later interpreters debate what the ritual actually was, whether it was actual child sacrifice or merely the passing of one's child through fire as a means of dedication.

lions.bio: Throughout the Bible, lions feature as symbols of strength and leadership, metaphors for enemies or danger, and actual creatures that play roles in biblical narratives. In the Talmud, the lion is referred to as “[king of the beasts](https://www.sefaria.org/Chagigah.13b.5?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en),” and rabbinic sources address questions like how to [assess damages](https://www.sefaria.org/Mishnah_Bava_Kamma.1.4?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) when a lion attacks. Since ancient times, lions have featured in Jewish imagery: lions [adorned](https://www.sefaria.org/I_Kings.7.29?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) vessels in the Temple in Jerusalem, lions have been featured on menorahs and other ritual objects and in synagogue art, and a lion is the emblem of the city of Jerusalem. T

neshama2.bio: The [light](/topics/light2) that is [enclothed](/topics/enclothe) in the [vessel](/topics/vessel1) of [*Bina*](/topics/bina3) is called “*neshama*,” in the sense of *neshima*, a breath, since *neshama* is mainly a source for [*Ze*’*er* *Anpin*](/topics/zeer-anpin1), which is an expression of light and spirit. It breathes in its life force from there, in a manner of ascent and descent, in the mystical meaning of the verse: “And the living creatures ran and returned” (Ezekiel 1:14), and as in the verse: “And He breathed into his nostrils the breath of life” (Genesis 2:7). This must be understood (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 2, Q & A 49).

pidyon-haben.bio: Pidyon HaBen ("redemption of the son") is a ceremony in which a 30-day old firstborn son is redeemed by transferring monetary objects to a [kohen](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/priesthood?tab=sources). The commandment is rooted in [Exodus 13:12-15](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.13.12?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he), which ties the practice to the Exodus story and God's sparing of Israelite firstborns when Egyptian firstborns were killed, and [Numbers 18:15-16](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.18.15?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he).

judith.bio: Judith is an apocryphal character. While she does not appear in the Tanakh, versions of her story are found both in the Apocrypha and Rabbinic literature. In the Book of Judith, she was a widow who snuck into the Assyrian camp and won over their general Holofernes before beheading him.

inner-light.bio: The inner light refers to the supernal light that successfully permeates and is [enclothed](/topics/enclothe) within every [level](/topics/level) of reality, in accordance with the [level](/topics/level)’s capacity to contain it at any given time. This contrasts with [surrounding light](/topics/surrounding-light), the light that reality cannot contain because it is too limited to contain the infinite divine light and so the surrounding light cannot shine within reality at present (but will do so in the future).

grapes.bio: Grapes are one of the [seven special agricultural products](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/seven-species?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) of the land of Israel. They appear in several instances in the Torah, like when [Noah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/noah?tab=admin) plants a vineyard and gets drunk after emerging from the ark, or when [spies](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-spies?tab=admin) bring back huge grapes from their expedition in the land of Israel. Later sources discuss legal questions relating to grapes, vineyards, and wine and analyze the qualities and significance of this fruit.

laughter.bio: The Bible uses a version of the Hebrew term for laughter – *tzechok* or *sechok* – 50 times, though in some instances, the meaning of the word can be interpreted as "play," "mock," or other ways. Rabbinic texts include many stories and teachings about laughter, and often present multiple perspectives on the merit of laughter: one source, for example, considers ["limitation of laughter"](https://www.sefaria.org/Pirkei_Avot.6.6?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) to be a way of acquiring the Torah, while another source calls tears of laughter ["beautiful."](https://www.sefaria.org/Shabbat.152a.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) Later Jewish writers consider and debate laughter's role in religious life, distinguishing between different types of laughter and different circumstances.

the-tabernacle-in-shiloh.bio: The Tabernacle in Shiloh (Mishkan Shiloh) was the main spiritual center for the Jews living in Israel before the Temple was built in Jerusalem. The Tabernacle was established in Shiloh after the conquest of Canaan and remained there until Shiloh was destroyed by the Philistines.

psukei-dezimrah.bio: Psukei Dezimrah (literally "verses of singing) is the introductory section of the daily prayers.

temple-mount.bio: The Temple Mount (*Har HaBayit*) is the hill in Jerusalem where the Temple once stood and, according to rabbinic literature, the site of several important events. Although the Temple no longer exists, the site retains holiness in Jewish law and thought. Today, the Dome of the Rock is built on the Temple Mount, and discussion persists as to where one is allowed to enter according to Jewish law.

levite-cities.bio: The Levitical Cities (Arei HaLeviim) were 48 cities that were set aside for the Levites who were not given their own land in Israel. Of those 48 cities, six also served as cities of refuge as defined in the Bible.

humor.bio: Several biblical passages feature laughter, [playfulness](https://www.sefaria.org/Psalms.104.26?lang=he), or [irony](https://www.sefaria.org/Esther?tab=contents), though the extent to which the Bible contains intentional humor is a matter of debate. In rabbinic literature, humor is utilized as a tool for [engaging](https://www.sefaria.org/Bekhorot.8b.16?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) with foreign views, as a [pedagogical method](https://www.sefaria.org/Shabbat.30b.6?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) to engage students, and as part of natural interactions between scholars. Chasidic texts expound on the significance of incorporating humor in one's spiritual life, and works throughout the Jewish textual tradition engage in humor and analyze its role in Jewish life.

mayim-achronim.bio: Water used for washing the hands at the end of a meal.

spring.bio: In the Torah, the Hebrew word *aviv* – translated as "spring" – refers to a specific stage in grain growth, often understood as the stage in which seeds reach their full size but have not yet dried. The word is also used as part of the phrase *"chodesh ha'aviv"* ("the month of spring"), which refers to the time period in which grain reaches this stage. The holiday of [Passover](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/passover?tab=admin) occurs during this season, and Jewish sources often refer to this time of year as one of renewal and redemption.

partitions.bio: A partition (mechitzah) is noted in Jewish law as a way to separate two domains. Partitions are discussed in the Talmud as a way to separate men from women during Sukkot celebrations in the Temple in Jerusalem.

moriah.bio: Mount Moriah (Har Hamoriah) is another name given to Jerusalem and is associated with the Temple Mount in the Bible. This is also the location where Abraham brought up Isaac as a sacrifice.

aliases: Yose b. Yohanan [a man] of Jerusalem
Yose b. Yochanan (a man) of Jerusalem
Yose ben Yohanan [a man] of Jerusalem
Yose ben Yochanan (a man) of Jerusalem
yose-b-yochanan.bio: Yose b. Yoezer and Yose b. Yochanan served as the first of the zugot (pairs), with the first being Nasi (religious leader) and the second the head of the court. They served after Antigonos in a period of intense Hellenization and war, and promulgated several leniencies to encourage Jews to keep the law under these difficult conditions.

vahavta.bio: VeAhavta — "you shall love — refers to the verses ([Deuteronomy 6:5–9](https://www.sefaria.org/Deuteronomy.6.5?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he)) that come after [Shema Yisrael](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shema?tab=sources) in the Torah and form an integral part of the Shema prayer. It relates the commandment of total love of God and how to live God's words through actions.

twelve-tribes.bio: The twelve tribes refer to twelve groups of Jacob's descendants, collectively known as the people of Israel. In some contexts, this refers to Jacob's twelve sons: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Asher, Issachar, Zebulun, Joseph, and Benjamin. In other contexts, like in the allocation of land in the land of Israel, the tribe of [Levi](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.2.33?lang=bi&with=Navigation&lang2=en) is not counted, and the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh – who were Joseph's sons – are listed [instead of a tribe of Joseph](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.48.5?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en).

shabbat-prayers.bio: Shabbat Prayers add psalms, hymns, and an additional service to the structure of the daily prayers. Sections of the Torah are read in the morning and afternoon and a section of the Prophets is read only in the morning. There is also a unique service welcoming it at night.

zav.bio: Zav is a halakhic term for a man with a genital discharge.

new-year.bio: There are four New Years in Jewish tradition: the New Year for governments and festivals in the spring, the New Year for tithing animals in late summer, the New Year for calendars and spiritual renewal (Rosh Hashanah) in the fall, and the New Year for trees and planting in the end of winter.

moses-chaim-luzzatto-(ramchal).bio: Moses Chaim Luzzatto (Ramchal) was an Italian rabbi, kabbalist, and philosopher who also wrote dramatic works and literary criticism. Gifted with an almost photographic memory, he wrote many works, some which became standards of Kabbalah and ethics. He was suspected of Sabbateanism but was exonerated by his teachers and colleagues with a warning to cease engaging in speculative kabbalistic writing. Toward the end of his life, he moved to the land of Israel.

abodes.bio: In Jewish law, it’s important to understand what constitutes an abode (dirah). For example, a sukkah (temporary hut) must be large enough to be considered an abode, and the same is true in order for a structure to require a mezuzah (parchment enclosed in a case on a doorpost).

choshen.bio: The choshen (breastplate) was one of eight priestly garments worn by the high priest in the Temple.

air.bio: In kabbalistic and rabbinic literature, air is described as one of [four elements](https://www.sefaria.org/Bamidbar_Rabbah.14.12?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=he) from which God created the world. Works of Jewish thought [discuss](https://www.sefaria.org/sheets/248511.25?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) the significance of air's essentialness and ubiquity, and rabbinic and halakhic texts discuss air's role in different areas of Jewish law: as a space in which [legal acquisitions](https://www.sefaria.org/Bava_Metzia.102a.11?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) can happen, for example, or as something that can [render a person impure](https://www.sefaria.org/Gittin.8b.3?lang=bi&with=Tosafot&lang2=en).

kippah.bio: A Kippah, or yarmulke, is a type of head covering worn in order to recite blessings or prayers. It also serves as a reminder and sign of honor to God.

diaphragm.bio: The diaphragm (*parsa*) is a term describing the separation between the world [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut) and the worlds below. This separation is a result of the [second constriction](/topics/second-constriction), in which [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), with its attendant first [constriction](/topics/constriction), ascended to the [level](/topics/level) of [*Bina*](/topics/bina3). In Kabbalah language, the structures of the [*partzufim*](/topics/partzuf) and the [levels](/topics/level) are described using terms derived from the human body. The diaphragm in the body lies at the point of the chest, similar to the location of the [level](/topics/level) of [*Bina*](/topics/bina3) within the [body](/topics/body3) of a [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1).

scorpions.bio: The scorpion is mentioned once in the Bible, as an example of a dangerous creature from which God protected the people of Israel during their time wandering in the desert. Rabbinic texts discuss conditions under which a scorpion can be killed on Shabbat and instances in which people [miraculously](https://www.sefaria.org/Pirkei_Avot.5.5?lang=bi) [avoided](https://www.sefaria.org/Shabbat.22a.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) harm from scorpions. Mystical sources explore the Zodiac sign Scorpio and its connection to the Hebrew month of Cheshvan.

ever-hayarden.bio: Ever HaYarden was a region on the eastern side of the Jordan River where the Israelite tribes of Gad and Reuven chose to reside. Because Ever HaYarden was not part of the land where the Israelites were meant to settle, it became a province with unique customs and laws.

tishrei.bio: The first month in the Jewish calendar and the seventh in the biblical calendar.

hitbodedut.bio: Hitbodedut is the Hebrew word for meditation. A practice that is attributed to the prophets and to other highly spiritual individuals after them, it has become popularized through the works and followers of Rabbi Nachman of Breslov.

bat-mitzvah.bio: A bat mitzvah is a coming of age ceremony, marked for Jewish girls at age 12 in Orthodox communities and age 13 in Reform, Conservative, and Reconstructionist communities. Rabbinic sources indicate that at age 12, girls become obligated in commandments  and accountable for their own actions. The practice of marking this transition with a religious ceremony for girls has traces in 19th-century Jewish literature, took root in the United States with the first public bat mitzvah in 1922, and started becoming more commonplace in the 1970s.

immaturity1.bio: The two *partzufin* of suckling [gestation](/topics/gestation) in each [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) are called immaturity, because of the lack of the [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) of the [brains](/topics/brains2), which is the [head](/topics/head2) of every [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 15, Q & A 119).

the-four-parshiot.bio: "The four parshiot" refers to four unique Torah selections that are read in synagogues, after the regular weekly Torah portion, before and throughout the Hebrew month of [Adar](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/adar?tab=sources). The four parshiot are: shekalim, read on the Shabbat before the month of Adar, zakhor, read on the Shabbat before the holiday of [Purim](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/purim?tab=sources), parah, generally read on the Shabbat after Purim, and hachodesh, read on the Shabbat before the Hebrew month of [Nisan](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/nisan?sort=Relevance&tab=sources).

infertility.bio: Stories and discussions of the challenges of infertility are common throughout the Jewish textual tradition. Each of the four biblical [matriarchs](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/the-matriarchs?tab=admin), as well as [other](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/hannah?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) [biblical](https://www.sefaria.org/II_Kings.4.14?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) [characters](https://www.sefaria.org/Judges.13.2?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en), struggle with infertility; their stories provide windows into the multi-faceted emotional aspects of the experience and different ways of reacting to it. Texts throughout the centuries address halakhic questions related to infertility – and, more recently, reproductive technology – as well as religious and ethical perspectives

yisrael-sabba-and-tevuna.bio: *Yisrael Sabba* and *Tevuna* is a double [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) with a male and female aspect. It was generated in the world of [*Atzilut*](/topics/atzilut) after the second constriction ([*tzimtzum*](/topics/tzimtzum1)), which separated every [level](/topics/level) of reality into two parts. The upper part of each [level](/topics/level) contains the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) of [*Keter*](/topics/keter1) and [*Hokhma*](/topics/hokhma1), while the lower part of the [level](/topics/level) contains [*Bina*](/topics/bina3)>, [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1), and [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1). This split began in the third [level](/topics/level) of [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-kadmon2), which is the [level](/topics/level) of [*Bina*](/topics/bina3)> of [*Adam Kadmon*](/topics/adam-

agunah.bio: An agunah ("chained women") is a woman who is halakhically stuck in a dead marriage because her husband is unable or refuses to give her a get, or bill of divorce.

languages.bio: Language is present from the beginning of the Bible, as God creates the world through utterances. Countless Jewish stories and texts subsequently discuss language and languages in a variety of contexts.

the-four-expressions-of-redemption.bio: “The Four Expressions of Redemption” refers to four expressions from God’s speech to Moses in Exodus 6:6–7, in which God promises to redeem the Israelites from their enslavement in Egypt. God uses four phrases to describe the process: “I will take you out,” “I will save you,” I will redeem you,” and “I will take you.” Some consider a phrase in the following verse, “I will bring you,” to be a fifth expression of redemption.

aliases: Rebbi Cohen the brother of Rebbi Hiyya b. Abba
rabbi-kohen.bio: Rav Kohen was the brother of R. Chiyya b. Abba, and also appeared to have moved from Babylonia to Israel. He is only mentioned a few times in rabbinic literature.

frogs.bio: In the Jewish textual tradition, frogs feature most notably in the [plague of frogs](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/plague-of-frogs?tab=admin) wrought upon the Egyptians in the book of [Exodus](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus?tab=contents). Later sources describe how these frogs developed and multiplied, analyze the frogs' traits, and even derive laws based on the actions of the frogs. Frogs are also featured in various stories and teachings throughout midrashic and rabbinic literature.

ruah1.bio:
*Ruaḥ* refers to the [light](/topics/light2) that is in the [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) of [*Ze*’*er* *Anpin*](/topics/zeer-anpin1), or the world of [*Yetzira*](/topics/yetzira1) ([Formation](/topics/yetzira1)).
For this reason, the [light of giving](/topics/light-of-giving) is called *ruaḥ* (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 1, Q & A 50) and the level of the [light](/topics/light2) of the *ruaḥ*, which is the [six extremities](/topics/six-extremities) (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 13, Q & A 106).

dirt.bio: Adam, the first human, is created from the "[dust of the ground](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.2.7?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he)," and Abraham is promised that his descendant will be as numerous as "[the dust of the earth](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.28.14?ven=hebrew|Tanach_with_Ta%27amei_Hamikra&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he)." Dust and dirt also play roles in many other stories, rituals, and laws throughout biblical and rabbinic literature, like [the commandment](https://www.sefaria.org/Leviticus.17?lang=he&aliyot=0) to cover blood of a slaughtered animal with earth and the role of dirt in the [*sotah*](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/sotah?tab=admin) ritual.

the-curtain.bio: The Curtain (Parochet) is the finely embroidered cloth that covers the Ark of the Covenant and divides the Temple’s inner sanctum from the rest of the shrine.

levi-b-sisi.bio: An important member of Rabi's entourage, Levi' b. Sisi's relationship with the house of the Nasi was complicated. A brilliant scholar, he also composed a version of the Mishnah. Yet due to his dissatisfaction with his treatment by the academy, he moved to Babylonia and established his own academy there.

birkot-hashachar.bio: The daily morning blessings — *birkot hashachar,* in Hebrew — acknowledge and thank God for all the blessings bestowed on people in general, and Jews in particular. Examples include appreciation for vision, strength, and for clothing.

the-idol-of-micha.bio: The idol of Micah refers to a story from the book of Judges, chapters 17 and 18, involving a statue and accompanying temple of a man named Micah, and the tribe of Dan's conquest of the city of Laish.

astronomy.bio: Astronomical calculations have long been an important element of Judaism, a religion with a lunar calendar with influence from the solar calendar. While constellations and zodiac signs appear often in Jewish literature, scholars and mystics alike emphasize their subordinance to God and note the wonder that the heavens can inspire as a reminder of the universe’s vastness and God’s creation.

beit-el.bio: Beit El, which means “house of God,” was a biblical town located in central Israel. Also known as Bethel, this ancient city is where Jacob had a dream of angels going up and down a ladder. Jacob built an altar to God in Beit El, but it later became a hub for idolatry.

plague-of-blood.bio: Water turning into blood was the first of ten plagues that God wrought upon the Egyptians when Pharoah refused to let the enslaved Israelites go. It is described in [Exodus 7:14—24](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.7.14?ven=The_Koren_Jerusalem_Bible&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en)

public-protests.bio: Protesting for righteous causes is a practice that appears throughout Jewish sources, from Abraham's appeal that God spare the righteous people of Sodom, to discussions in rabbinic literature about the need to protest when one witnesses transgressions being committed.

the-nile-river.bio: The Nile River was essential to life in Egypt, serving as the main source of the country's water supply. It was also revered and worshiped by the Egyptians. Notably, this river was where the Egyptians cast Jewish infants during their oppression, and it played a significant role in the Passover story when it turned to blood.

the-four-rivers.bio: The Four Rivers refer to the rivers that flow out of the Garden of Eden, mentioned in [Genesis 2:10-14](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.2.10?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=he&with=all&lang2=he). They are generally understood to refer to the Tigris, Euphrates, Nile (or Ganges), and Blue Nile rivers.

beersheba.bio: Beersheba, which translates as “well of the seven” or “well of the oath,” is a city located in southern Israel. The name likely comes from the oath that Abraham made there to the Philistine King Abimelech or to the seven wells dug in the area by Isaac.

tashlich.bio: Tashlikh (casting away) is a tradition observed on Rosh Hashanah afternoon or anytime between Rosh Hashanah and [Hoshana Rabbah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/hoshana-rabbah?sort=Relevance&tab=sources). Participants recite specific prayers by water, seeking divine forgiveness by symbolically shaking out their garments and casting away their sins into the depths of the waters.

nephilim.bio: *Nephilim* are biblical figures that appear twice in the Torah: first, in [Genesis 6:4](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.6.4?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en), in a verse that describes the unions of “*bnei elohim*” and “daughters of men,” and then in [Numbers 13:33](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.13.33?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en), when 12 spies report that they saw *nephilim* in the land of Canaan. *Nephilim* also appear [once](https://www.sefaria.org/Ezekiel.32.27?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=bi) in the book of Ezekiel according to some understandings, and in several instances throughout works of Apocrypha. In the Jewish textual tradition, these creatures are understood to be angels, the offspring

travel.bio: "From its very inception, the Jewish people has always been a nation of travelers," [writes](https://www.sefaria.org/Contemporary_Halakhic_Problems%2C_Vol_III%2C_Part_I%2C_Chapter_II_Travelers.4?lang=bi&with=About&lang2=en) Rabbi J. David Bleich in his work, *Contemporary Halakhic Problems.* Throughout the Torah, biblical figures travel generally because of a divine command – Abraham goes forth when God tells him to, the people of Israel travel in the desert for forty years because God decrees it, and the Torah instructs people to make a pilgrimage to Jerusalem for the festivals. Biblical travel also occurs because of necessity, such as when famine strikes. Jewish liturgy includes a prayer for safe travels, and rabbinic and contemporary sources delve into questions surrounding trav

rabbi-yehudah-nesia-(ii).bio: R. Yehudah Nesi'a II was the grandson of R. Yehudah Nesi'a I. He studied under R. Yochanan and depended upon him and R. Ami to come to many of his decisions. He promoted Torah education at a time when it was in decline.

syria.bio: Syria holds a unique place in Jewish history due to its conquest by King David and its temporary affiliation with the Jewish commonwealth. Jewish sources portray Syria as a region that occupies a middle ground—not as sanctified as the land of Israel yet not entirely detached from it either.

rabbi-tzadok-(i).bio: R. Tzadok was an outstanding scholar during the time of R. Yochanan b. Zakkai. He is most famous for having fasted for forty years to avert the destruction of the Temple. He was healed by a Roman physician procured by Rabban Yochanan.

terminating-malkhut.bio: When the [partition](/topics/partition2) that is above the [level](/topics/level) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) prevent the supernal light, or direct light, entering it, yet a [fusion through collision](/topics/fusion-through-collision) does not occur there, this [level](/topics/level) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) is known as the terminating *Malkhut*. It is a terminating [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) because it constitutes the end of the [level](/topics/level).

the-concubine-of-givah.bio: The "Concubine of Givah" was a woman from Bethlehem in Judah who married a Levite husband, whom she sought to leave and return to her father's house. Her husband comes to retrieve her. Tragedy and civil war that nearly destroy the tribe of Benjamin ensues. The story begins in [Judges 19](https://www.sefaria.org/Judges.19.2?lang=bi).

ovadiah-bartenura.bio: Ovadiah of Bartenura was an Italian rabbi, banker, commentator on the Mishnah, and community leader. He studied under [Maharik Kolon](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/joseph-colon?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) in Bologna. In 1485, upon the death of this wife, he began a two-and-a-half-year journey to the land of Israel. He visited many communities along the way and eventually settled in Jerusalem, where he quickly rose to prominence and labored unceasingly to restore Jewish communal life, both material and spiritual, and was largely successful in his efforts. He reestablished a chevrah kaddisha (Jewish burial society), in which he actively served, and a yeshiva, upon the arrival of learned Spanish exiles. He was recognized as a halakhic authority, was recognized as the chief rabbi

flour.bio: Flour (kemach) appears in Rabbinic discussions because of its importance in the production of bread and in Temple offerings. Flour is also used as a metaphor for earning a wage: “If there is no flour, there is no Torah. if there is no Torah, there is no flour” (Pirkei Avot 3:17).

fast-of-gedaliah.bio: Tzom Gedaliah ("the Fast of Gedaliah") is a minor fast day on the third of the Hebrew month of Tishrei, which is the day after Rosh HaShanah. It is observed from dawn to nightfall and commemorates the assasination of Gedaliah, a Judean official, by fellow Judeans — an act that led to further dispersal during the Babylonian Exile.

journey-of-the-ark.bio: Journey of the Ark in the desert.

rashbam.bio: Shmuel ben Meir (Rashbam) was a French Tosafist and Torah commentator. He was a son of Rashi's daughter, Yocheved, and older brother of the famous Tosafists, Isaac ben Meir (Rivam) and Jacob ben Meir (Rabbeinu Tam). His Torah commentary is concise, and hews strictly to the concept of the "peshat" or plain-sense meaning of the text, sometimes at the expense of received rabbinic traditions. He does not hesitate to argue with Rashi when he feels that his commentary strayed from the plain meaning of the verse. Rashbam also wrote a lengthier commentary on the Talmud, portions of which are printed in the Vilna Shas where no commentary of Rashi is available. Rashbam's opinions are also frequently mentioned in the Tosafot throughout Shas.

sheva-berakhot.bio: The sheva berakhot ("seven blessings") were established to celebrate a marriage, invoking the happy fulfillment of God's plan through marriage. They are recited at the wedding itself and at most festive meals eaten by first-time brides and grooms during their first week of marriage.

rainbows.bio: In the Jewish textual tradition, the rainbow features most prominently in [Genesis chapter 9](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.9.12?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en), when God tells [Noah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/noah?tab=sources) that the rainbow is a symbol of God's covenant between God and all living things, demonstrating that God will never again destroy the world with a flood. Jewish liturgy includes a [blessing](https://www.sefaria.org/Weekday_Siddur_Sefard_Linear%2C_Berachos_Said_Upon_Witnessing_Phenomenal_Sights.9?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) to be said upon the sight of a rainbow, invoking a reminder of God's covenant. Generations of Jewish scholars have expounded upon the rainbow's significance, origins, and messages.

ten-martyrs-of-the-empire.bio: Jewish tradition associates the brutal killing of ten great Torah scholars by the Romans with punishment of Joseph's brothers for selling him. Though not all at the same time, the executions were all around the time of the Hadrianic persecutions aimed at crushing Jewish opposition to Roman rule. The Ten Martyrs included R. Yishmael, Rabban [Shimon b.] Gamliel, R. Yeshavav, R. Yehudah b. Bava, R. Chutzpit the Interpreter, R. Yehuda the Baker, R. Hananyah b. Teradion and R. Akiva.

pits.bio: In ancient Israel, people commonly dug pits (bor) in order to collect rainwater, which made pits part of the landscape. In the Jewish sources, there is much discussion about who is responsible for damages caused by pits.

alexandria.bio: Alexandria was founded by Alexander the Great in the 4th century BCE and served as the capital of Egypt until the Muslim conquest of the 7th century CE. As the intellectual and cultural hub of the ancient Mediterranean world, it became a major Jewish center of life and learning.

shalom-bayit.bio: The term *shalom bayit* — “domestic peace” or “peace in the home” — in talmudic literature refers to a pleasant and peaceful atmosphere in one’s home. Later sources extend the term to refer to harmony between spouses, a value seen as so important that [God was willing to have God’s holy name erased](https://www.sefaria.org/Nedarim.66b.2?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he) for its sake. A home with a strong bond between spouses is considered a space where [God’s presence rests](https://www.sefaria.org/Sotah.17a.15?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=he).

tazria-metzora.bio: Tazria-Metzora is a double Torah portion that describes different forms of tzaraat, a discoloration condition that affects skin, clothing, or houses, and purification for the infected. It also addresses the ritual impurity generated by certain bodily discharges.

lekha-dodi1.bio: [Lekha Dodi](https://www.sefaria.org/Lekha_Dodi.1?lang=bi) ("Come, My Beloved") is a liturgical poem written by 16th-century Safed kabbalist, [Shlomo Alkabetz](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shlomo-halevi-alkabetz?tab=popular-writing-of). The poem, which is set to many different tunes, serves as the centerpiece of the Kabbalat Shabbat liturgy to welcome Shabbat every Friday night. With deeply mystical undertones, it refers to God as the Jews' companion who will join them to greet Shabbat.

darkhei-shalom.bio: Darchei Shalom (lit. "Ways of Peace") refers to good relations among neighbors.

gemorrah.bio: Gemorrah (Amorah) was a city near Sodom that shared the evil inclinations and fate of total destruction that Sodom faced.

jehoiachin.bio: This king of Judah, whose name means "the Lord will establish" is known in Tanakh by the name Jeconiah (Yekhoniah). He was one of the last kings of the Southern Kingdom of Judah and only reigned for three months before he was taken into Babylonian exile in 597 BCE by the [Nebuchadnezzar](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/nebuchadnezzar?tab=sources), the king of Babylon.

summer.bio: The Torah first mentions that concept of summer in the book of [Genesis](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.8.22?lang=bi&aliyot=0) after the story of the [flood](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/flood). The Bible also [uses](https://www.sefaria.org/Jeremiah.40.10?lang=bi) the Hebrew word for summer, *kayitz,* to refer to a type of fruit, likely dried dates. In rabbinic literature, the summer months are occasionally referred to as "*tekufat Tammuz,*" which means the period beginning with the Hebrew month of Tammuz (and ending at the end of the Hebrew month of Elul). Various laws and customs are associated with seasons and seasonal changes.

rabbi-yose-b-kisma.bio: A moderate scholar who lived during the Hadrianic persecutions, R Yose b. Kisma advocated accomodation with the Romans.

damascus.bio: As one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world, Damascus is mentioned many times in the Bible and other Jewish sources. Damascus is sometimes synonymous with Aram, an Aramean state, in the Jewish sources. Today, Damascus is the capital of Syria.

wolves.bio: With a natural habitat including much of the Middle East and Europe, wolves have been part of Jewish life and literature since biblical times. They are carnivorous predators, and represent a particular threat to flock animals. Wolves appear in the Tanakh (Hebrew Bible), especially in books of prophecy, as an analogy for human viciousness, and, because of [Jacob’s blessing](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.49.27) at the end of his life, are a symbol for the tribe of Benjamin. In an idyllic vision of the messianic era, [Isaiah describes](https://www.sefaria.org/Isaiah.11.6) the wolf cohabiting peacefully with its natural prey, the lamb.

kol-nidrei4.bio: [Kol Nidrei](https://www.sefaria.org/Machzor_Yom_Kippur_Ashkenaz_Linear%2C_Kol_Nidrei.1?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) is a text commonly recited in synagogues at the opening of [Yom Kippur](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yom-kippur?tab=sources) services. It consists of a declaration that all vows and prohibitions that one has taken upon themself should be nullified.

moses-prayer.bio: Moses' Prayer consists of the many times that he successfully intercedes on behalf of others. He does this to deflect God's anger at the sins of the entire people or of specific individuals like Aaron and Miriam. Moses appeals to God's attribute of mercy, using various arguments.

yehida1.bio: The [light](/topics/light2) enclothed in the [*sefira*](/topics/sefira2) of [*Keter*](/topics/keter1) is called *yeḥida* (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 2, Q & A 31).

bachya-ibn-pekuda.bio: Bachya ibn Pekudah was a Spanish rabbi, philosopher, and moralist who lived in Saragossa, where he served as a judge on the Jewish court. In addition to a comprehensive knowledge of Jewish literature, he had a wide knowledge of secular literature and frequently quoted non-Jewish moral philosophers. He wrote his great work, Chovot HaLevavot ("Duties of the Heart"), to fill what he perceived as a need for bringing together the many ethical teachings scattered throughout Jewish literature in a systematic work. He argued forcefully for investing in the inner content of Jewish practice, as opposed to singularly focusing on outward observance. His works displayed the rare combination of tremendous emotion, vivid poetic imagination, powerful eloquence, and a penetrating intellect.

book-of-life.bio: The phrase "book of life" – also translated as "book of the living" – appears once in the Bible, in the book of [Psalms](https://www.sefaria.org/Psalms.69.29?lang=bi). Perhaps on the basis of this verse, the [Talmud describes](https://www.sefaria.org/Rosh_Hashanah.16b.12?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) how, on the holiday of Rosh Hashanah, three books are opened before God: one for the righteous, one for the wicked, and one for the intermediate. The righteous, the Talmud goes on to explain, are inscribed in "the book of life." This talmudic passage is reflected in much of the liturgy of the High Holidays, including in the [Unetaneh Tokef](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/unetaneh-tokef?tab=sources) prayer.

13-principles-of-faith.bio: Written as part of Rambam's Commentary on the Mishnah to the tenth chapter of tractate Sanhedrin, Rambam's 13 Principles of Faith articulate the fundamental tenets of Jewish faith. Rambam wanted to help people avoid errors of faith. The 13 Principles are first found as declarative statements of faith ("I believe with perfect faith that...") in the Venice Hagaddah of 1566 CE and most popularly known from the poem, Yigdal, from around 1300 CE.

pomegranates.bio: Pomegranates appear in assorted contexts in the Jewish textual tradition. They are perhaps most prominently associated with the holiday of Rosh HaShanah, but also feature in various biblical, rabbinic, and philosophical contexts.

pharisees.bio: The Pharisee party consisted of those loyal to tradition, and who developed rabbinic scholarship that further explained many of the laws and teachings of the Torah. They struggled to wrest power away from the rival Sadducees during the Hasmonean period. Their sincerity and piety however won over most of the Jews, such that they were eventually victorious in determining the course of subsequent Jewish history.

kislev.bio: Kislev is the third month of the Hebrew calendar, during which the holiday of [Chanukkah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/chanukkah?tab=sources) is celebrated.

mah-tovu.bio: Mah Tovu -- "how good" -- is a prayer recited at the outset of the daily morning service. Its opening line is from the book of Numbers, when the non-Israelite prophet Balaam sets out to curse the Israelites but ends up blessing them. In one such blessing, Balaam looks out over the Israelites' encampment and declares "[How good are your tents, Jacob, your dwellings, Israel!](https://www.sefaria.org/Numbers.24.5?ven=Tanakh:_The_Holy_Scriptures,_published_by_JPS&lang=en&aliyot=0)"

equating-of-form.bio: When discussing intangible spiritual [levels](/topics/level) or entities, the concepts of space and physical proximity do not apply. Spiritual entities and [levels](/topics/level) have different conceptual forms, or “*tzura*,” referring to the parameters or characteristics of a [level](/topics/level) or aspect. When a spiritual entity adapts its nature so that it resembles another entity, the two entities are said to have achieved an equating of form. This equating of form creates a state called *devekut*, bonding, attachment, or merging one with the other. While in the physical world, two entities are attached or joined through physical proximity, in the nonspatial spiritual realm, attachment is achieved through the equating of form.

gematria.bio: Gematria is the practice of assigning numerical values to Hebrew letters. Throughout rabbinic and Jewish literature, Gematria is used as a tool for interpreting biblical texts and deriving meaning from words and phrases.

tabernacle-courtyard.bio: The Tabernacle Courtyard (Hatzer Hamishkan) was the area surrounding the Tabernacle sanctuary and outwardly demarcated by curtains. The sacrificial altar, which stood outside of the sanctuary, was also in the courtyard.

mah1.bio: *Mah,* or *mem-heh*, is the numerical value of 45 in Hebrew letters (*mem*=40; *heh*=5). This numerical value is derived from the letters of the Tetragrammaton, the four-letter name of God referred to as the name of [Havaya](/topics/havaya2), when they are spelled out in full using the letter *alef*. In other words, each of the letters of the name of [Havaya](/topics/havaya2) is written out as a separate word, and the letter *alef* is used to fill out the letters, as follows: The *yod* is spelled out as *yod-vav-dalet* (יוד), the *heh* is spelled *heh-alef* (הא), the *vav* is spelled *vav-alef-vav* (ואו), and the final *heh* is spelled *heh-alef* (הא). *Mah* represents the [level](/topics/level) of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1) or the light of [*Tiferet*](/topics/tiferet1).

peppers.bio: Pepper (pilpel) was well-known during Talmudic times and the word referred to both black peppercorns and the long pepper found in India today. In the Talmud (Berakhot 36), there is a lengthy discussion about the blessing to make on the Indian long pepper.

kitniyot.bio: There is much debate in the Talmud about eating kitniyot on Passover. Generally translated as “legumes,” kitniyot includes rice, corn, sesame, and more. Jews are forbidden from eating leavened foods on Passover, but most Ashkenazi communities also forbid kitniyot.

lamed.bio: Lamed is the twelth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.

yahrzeit.bio: A yahrzeit is the annual anniversary commemoration of a person's death. The term is Yiddish and literally means anniversary.

tevet.bio: Tevet is the fourth month of the rabbinic calendar.

rabbi-ami-and-rabbi-asi.bio: R. Ami and R. Asi had studied together in Babylonia before coming to study with R. Yochanan. They excelled and eventually became the leaders of his academy in Tiberias, after R. Elazar b. Pedat. They assisted R. Yehudah Nesi'a II with their scholarship in the administration of the community.

nun.bio: Nun is the fourteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.

hay.bio: Hay is the fifth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.

naamah.bio: Na’amah is a biblical character mentioned in Genesis 4:22, in the context of a short genealogy of Cain’s descendants. She is identified there as the sister of Tubal-Kain, who “forged all implements of copper and iron” - a phrase commonly understood to mean that he was a founder of weaponry. In rabbinic literature, Na’amah is the name of Noah’s wife, but whether that is the same Na’amah as described in Genesis 4:22 is a matter of rabbinic debate.

zilpah.bio: Zilpah is a biblical character first introduced in [Genesis 29:24](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.29.24?lang=bi&with=all&lang2=en) as Laban's maidservant, whom he gives to his daughter Leah upon Leah's marriage to Jacob. When Leah sees that she has stopped bearing children, she gives Zilpah to Jacob so that Zilpah can bear children. Zilpah gives birth to two sons, Gad and Asher.

israel-meir-kagan.bio: Rabbi Israel Meir (HaKohen) Kagan, also known as the Chafetz Chaim, was one of the foremost leaders of Ashkenazi Jewry, whose seminal works, Sefer Chafetz Chaim and Shemirat HaLashon, have come to define the rules of proper speech. He also composed the Mishnah Berurah, a popular six-volume commentary on the first section of the Shulchan Arukh, in addition to many smaller works on Jewish law and ethics. He served briefly as the rabbi of Radun (Poland/Belarus) but resigned in order to found the Radun Yeshiva, which became world famous under his leadership.

maharal.bio: Judah Loew ben Betzalel, better known as Maharal of Prague, was a Bohemian rabbi, talmudist, mystically inclined philosopher, and community leader, whose writings left an indelible impression on both his own generation and subsequent generations of Ashkenazic Jewry. His numerous philosophical works became cornerstones of Jewish thought and had a profound influence on Chasidic teaching and some of the most prominent thinkers of modern Orthodoxy, including Rav Kook. He also wrote halakhic works, including a commentary on the Arba'ah Turim. He had a close relationship with the famous astronomer Tycho Brahe. Among his students were Rabbi Yom Tov Lipmann Heller (known as, the Tosefot Yom Tov) and the historian Rabbi David Ganz (Tzemach David). In popular literature or later centuries, he is fam

midwives.bio: Midwives feature in a variety of contexts in the Jewish textual tradition. In the Book of Exodus, the midwives Shifra and Puah defy Pharaoh’s orders to kill all Israelite baby boys, instead keeping them alive. Rabbinic sources analyze topics like under what conditions a midwife can violate the Sabbath, and various responsa incorporate medical knowledge provided by midwives into the process of legal decision making.

bet.bio: Bet is the second letter of the Hebrew alphabet.

queen-of-sheba.bio: The Queen of Sheba appears briefly in 1 Kings, where she meets King Solomon. This short, lightly narrated encounter has inspired many imaginative, interpretive elaborations to fill in the biblical account's sparse detail.

al-hanisim.bio: *Al HaNissim* ("For the Miracles") is a prayer added to the [*Amidah*](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shemoneh-esrei?sort=Relevance&tab=admin) (standing prayer) and the [Grace After Meals](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/birkat-hamazon?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) on [Purim](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/purim?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources) and [Chanukkah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/chanukkah?tab=admin). The prayer starts with a line recognizing God's miracles. It continues with unique texts for each of the two holidays, detailing the dangers Jews faced from the decrees of [Haman](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/haman?tab=admin) and Antiochus, respectively, and the miraculous deliverances.

yom-hashoah.bio: Yom HaShoah, also known as Holocaust Remembrance Day, is Israel's official day of commemorating the six million Jews murdered in the Holocaust and the Jewish resistance during that period. It is usually observed on the 27th day of the Hebrew month of Nissan. The day is marked by ceremonies, services, air raid sirens in Israel during which people silently reflect, and special prayers.

tzipporah.bio: Tzipporah is a biblical character mentioned in the book of Exodus as the wife of [Moses](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/moses) and one of seven daughters of the Midianite priest [Yitro](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yitro). She is mentioned only a few times in the Bible, but the rabbis further explored her character in midrashic literature.

snow.bio: Snow appears in Jewish texts in various contexts, both literal and figurative. Its white appearance is utilized as a metaphor in scripture, and snow is portrayed as part of God's creation in biblical and midrashic texts. There are also technical discussions about snow and ritual purity.

prozbul.bio: A contract arranged with the court that allows for debt extension past the Sabbatical year.

tammuz.bio: Tammuz is the tenth month of the Jewish calendar. It falls in the summer months.

naphtali-zevi-yehudah-berlin.bio: Naphtali Zvi Yehudah Berlin (Netziv) was a Lithuanian rabbi and one of the greatest scholars of his time. He was born into a family of Jewish scholars, a descendant on his mother's side of [Meir Eisenstadt](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/meir-eisenstadt). His first wife was the granddaughter of R. Chaim Volozhin, and his second the daughter of [R. Yechiel Michel Epstein](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yechiel-michel-epstein). In 1854, he was appointed head of the Volozhin yeshiva, where he introduced a style of Talmud study based on broad knowledge of tannaitic and geonic literature as well as the rishonim. He also emphasized the importance of the study of Torah and the Prophets, giving a daily shiur on the weekly Torah reading. In 1894, the Russian government forced him to close the yeshi

cheshvan.bio: Cheshvan is the second month of the Hebrew calendar, also sometimes called Mar-Cheshvan or "bitter Cheshvan" because there are no holidays during this month.

av.bio: Av is the eleventh month of the Jewish calendar.

geniva.bio: Geniva was a scholar who interacted with Rav in Babylonia. He was involved in a dispute with the powerful Mar Ukva, and it appears that he was eventually executed by the local authorities as a result.

tarshish.bio: Tarshish was an ancient city or region across the Mediterranean Sea from Israel, although its exact location is unknown. In the Bible, Tarshish was named as a source of King Solomon's great wealth in metals, and it was also the destination of Jonah’s boat when he fled from God.

honey.bio: In traditional Jewish texts, the Hebrew word “devash” sometimes refers to honey from bees and sometimes to the syrup of fruit, especially dates. Honey features in poetic and wisdom texts as an exemplar of sweetness, including as [an allegory](https://www.sefaria.org/Proverbs.24.13-14?lang=bi) for the sweetness of wisdom or Torah study, and the land of Israel is often described as a “[land flowing with milk and honey](https://www.sefaria.org/Exodus.3.8?lang=bi&aliyot=1).” Bee honey is very unusual for being a substance that is kosher despite coming from a non-kosher animal.

iyar.bio: Iyyar is the eighth month of the Jewish calendar.

geniza.bio: A genizah is storage for holy items that are no longer usable, such as torn parchments or other ritual objects that are worn out beyond use.

rabbi-yehudah-b-betera-(ii).bio: R. Yehudah b. Betera was a prominent teacher with an academy in Netzivin in Babylonia. He seems to have traveled a great deal and had prominent visitors come to him as well.

ushpizin.bio: Ushpizin ("guests") refers to a custom of the Jewish holiday of [Sukkot](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/sukkot?tab=sources), according to which each night of the holiday, a different historical guest is figuratively invited to the [sukkah](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/sukkah?tab=sources). The custom is kabbalistic in origin, and many recite a prayer formally inviting the guests. Traditionally, the seven guests are: Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, Moses, Aaron, and David. Some modern versions modify the list or add other guests as well.

yod.bio: Yod is the tenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.

yehudah-b-hiyya.bio: R. Chiyya's two sons, Yehudah b. Chiyya and Chizkiyah b. Chiyya, came with him from Babylonia to Palestine as young adults. Already known for their scholarship, they added distinction to Rabbi's academy. They died prematurely and were buried on either side of their father.

yom-yerushalayim.bio: Yom Yerushalayim, or Jerusalem Day, is a day of celebration marking the establishment of Israeli control over the Old City of Jerusalem and renewed access to Jewish holy sites.

adam-kadmon2.bio: The first world that receives from [*Ein Sof*](/topics/ein-sof1). It is also called “one line,” extending immediately after the [constriction](/topics/constriction) ([*tzimtzum*](/topics/tzimtzum1)) from [*Ein Sof*](/topics/ein-sof1) until near this world. The name “Adam” refers only to the [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot) of the straight line in the first world, which is the [light](/topics/light2) of the [*ruaḥ*](/topics/ruah1), meaning the [light](/topics/light2) of giving, not its circular [*sefirot*](/topics/sefirot), which contain only the [light](/topics/light2) of [*nefesh*](/topics/nefesh), meaning the [light](/topics/light2) of receiving for themselves, without the ability to give to others. This is the root of man (“Adam”) in this world (*Talmud Eser Sefirot*, v. 2, Q & A 1).

mordechai1.bio: 13th century German scholar, author of a Halachic compendium bearing the same name. Mordechai was a primary student of Maharam M'Rotenberg and a son-in-law of R' Yechiel of Paris. His work, known simply as "The Mordechai", is a compilation of Halakhic rulings and customs enacted by the great sages of that and preceding generations. It follows the order of Rif's "Sefer Halachot", and is printed immediately after the Rif in the Vilna Shas. Being that the author collected the material but did not actually publish it in final form, there are two vastly different versions of this work — the Rhenish edition and the Austrian edition. Our editions of Talmud contain the Rhenish version, which is the shorter of the two. Mordechai was martyred together with his entire family in 1298 during the "Rhind

baal-shem-tov.bio: Yisroel (Israel) ben Eliezer, better known as the Ba'al Shem Tov or by the acronym Besht, was the founder of the Chasidut. Of humble and obscure origins, he was introduced to the secrets of Kabbalah by Rabbi Adam Ba'al Shem of Ropczyce and other masters of practical Kabbalah. In 1734, he presented himself to the world as a ba'al shem, a "Master of the (divine) Name," the title used for holy men who could work miracles. His teachings made Kabbalah more accessible to the common Jew and emphasized ways of drawing closer to the Divine beyond the traditional framework of Torah study. As leader of the nascent Chasidic movement, he gained followers, most notably Rabbi [Dov Baer](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/dov-baer-of-mezeritch-(maggid-of-mezeritch)?sort=Relevance&tab=sources), known as th

zavah.bio: Zav is a halakhic term for a woman with a genital discharge.

beitar.bio: Beitar was an ancient city that was the final stronghold of the Jews during the Bar Kokhba revolt of the second century CE in which Jews tried — unsuccessfully — to end Roman rule. The Romans captured and destroyed the fortress and massacred all of its inhabitants.

machloket-lshem-shamayim.canonical: Arguments for the Sake of Heaven
aliases: Arguments for the Sake of Heaven
machloket-lshem-shamayim.bio: An argument for the sake of heaven — a *machloket leshem shamayim* — is a disagreement over a point of Torah law or ethics, debated with pure intentions. In Jewish thought, while disagreements can be destructive, disagreements over matters of Torah help sharpen our understanding of God's will and how to understand and live according to the Torah. The classic examples of these kinds of disagreements are those between the late Second Temple era sages  [Hillel](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/hillel?tab=admin) and [Shammai](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/shammai?tab=admin).

prayer-of-abraham.bio: The Prayer of Abraham was his plea to God not to destroy the evil city of Sodom. This was a manifestation of his great loving kindness towards all people, which brought him to challenge God about His intentions there.

sharon.bio: Sharon translates as “plain” and is the name given to the land between the Samarian Hills and Israel’s coast. The Sharon is most famously mentioned in the Bible in Shir Hashirim, or the Song of Songs.

יום-הזכרון-לחללי-מערכות-ישראל.canonicalHe: יום הזיכרון לחללי מערכות ישראל ופעולות האיבה
יום-הזכרון-לחללי-מערכות-ישראל.bio: Yom HaZikaron is Israel's official remembrance day for fallen soldiers and civilian victims of terrorism. It is usually observed on the fourth day of the Hebrew month of Iyyar, the day preceding [Yom HaAtzmaut](https://www.sefaria.org/topics/yom-haatzmaut?sort=Relevance&tab=notable-sources). The day is marked by ceremonies, services, special prayers, and air raid sirens in Israel during which people silently reflect.

aleinu.bio: The concluding reading of prayer services, Aleinu (Upon us) reminds Jews of their historical and universal mission. It thus provides transition from the lofty world of prayer to the world of human activity. Aleinu consists of two paragraphs, traditionally associated with Joshua.

germany.bio: Jews first found refuge in Germany after escaping Roman persecution. Over time, Jews both suffered and flourished in the country, with the first Reform synagogue opening in Germany in 1810. Before World War II, Germany was home to Central Europe’s largest Jewish community.

rabban-shimon-b-gamliel-(ii).bio: The son of Rabban Gamliel of Yavneh, Rabban Shimon b Gamliel (II) fled the Hadrianic persecutions in Palestine, which led to a hiatus in the office of the Nasi. Upon Hadrian's death and the lightening of the persecutions, he returned to the helm of the Sanhedrin in Usha. Like his father, he was vigorous in consolidating leadership and keeping the memory of Temple rituals alive.

tuesday.bio: The third day of creation in the Torah, generally associated with Tuesday, is the only day for which the biblical text repeats the phrase "[and God saw that it was good](https://www.sefaria.org/Genesis.1.12?lang=he&with=all&lang2=he)" twice. Some Jewish communities thus view the day as particularly auspicious. Tuesday also holds significance in select areas of Jewish law, thought, and biblical interpretation.

meribah.bio: Meribah is a place mentioned in the books of Exodus and Numbers. In one story in Exodus, the Israelites complain about a lack of water. God instructs Moses to hit a rock; Moses does, and water comes out. The place is then called Massah and Meribah, commemorating the incident. In a similar story described in Numbers, God instructs Moses to take his staff and talk to a rock after the Israelites complain about a lack of water. Moses hits the rock twice and water comes out. God then tells Moses and Aaron that they will not enter Israel, and refers to the water as "water of Meribah."

halbanat-panim.bio: Halbanat Panim (whitening of the face) refers to what happens when one is embarrassed by another: Their face turns pale with shame. Rabbinic sources say that embarrassing another is so terrible that one who shames another in public loses their share in the World to Come.

aliases: Rebbi Judah the Prince, grandson of Rebbi Judah the Prince
Rebbi Judah the Prince, grandson of Rebbi Judah
Rabbi Yehuda Nesia b. Rabban Gamliel b. Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi
Rabbi Yehuda Nesia b. Rabban Gamliel
rabbi-yehudah-nesia-(i).bio: R. Yehudah Nesi'a was the last Nasi to be both leader of the Academy and of the Jewish community in Palestine. Grandson of R. and son of Rabban Gamliel III, he emerged as a powerful leader at a difficult time. Mistrust of the aristocratic and lenient ways of the academy were growing, such that R. Yehudah often quelled challenges from within.

nahum-of-gamzo.bio: Nachum of Gamzo was a pious scholar who lived during the time of R. Yochanan b. Zakkai and was one of R. Akiva's teachers. He is most famous for his faith in God, regardless of obstacles in his path. He is therefore associated with the phrase he used for these obstacles, gam zo letova (this too is for the good).

rabbi-hananyah-b-teradyon.bio: Rabbi Hananyah ben Teradyon studied Torah under Rabbi Yose ben Kisma until Rabbi Yose felt it was counterproductive. Alongside Rabbi Elazar ben Parta, they were captured by the Romans. Rabbi Hananyah predicted that Rabbi Elazar would be saved due to his extensive acts of kindness, whereas Rabbi Hananyah himself excelled primarily in Torah study. Rabbi Hananyah was the father of Bruriah, who became the wife of Rabbi Meir.

matanot-levyonim.bio: Meaning "gifts for the poor," mattanot laevyonim refer to the commandment of giving to charity on the holiday of Purim. .

rabbi-yonatan-b-uziel.bio: Yonatan ben Uziel is considered to have been Hillel’s greatest student. The Talmud describes how his Torah study was so intense that birds flying above him during his studies would burn. He is credited with translating and explaining the Prophets in Aramaic.

fusing-malkhut.bio: The [level](/topics/level) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) that exists in every [*partzuf*](/topics/partzuf1) contains two aspects. One is the fusing *Malkhut*. When there is a [fusion through collision](/topics/fusion-through-collision) in the [partition](/topics/partition2) that is positioned above the [level](/topics/level) of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1), where the supernal light is rebuffed by the [partition](/topics/partition2) because it prevents the supernal light from entering this [level](/topics/level), the light that rises from it [enclothes](/topics/enclothe) the supernal light, thereby producing the next [level](/topics/level). The second aspect of [*Malkhut*](/topics/malkhut1) is the [terminating *Malkhut*](/topics/terminating-malkhut), where the [partition](/topics/partit

rav-kahana-(ii).bio: Rav Kahana was an important student of Rav in Babylonia. He executed another Jew based on Jewish law, and hence had to flee to Israel. He then studied with R. Yochanan, who voiced his great admiration for him after initially misjudging him and thinking that he was disrespectful.
